### persistence — 2014-03-12T05:50:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-ribose.9143/post-114954>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=103891)
>
> I have experimented extensively up to 100 grams per day with no ill effects if I keep up the magnesium and CoQ. The GRAS (Generally Regarded As Safe) level is 20 grams per day. My wife baked Christmas cakes with it and they are wonderful. I recommend powdering it in a coffee grinder before mixing it in recipes, seems to work better. It is very hydrophilic which means it dissolves very easily in polar solvents such as water and milk. Also seems to mix well with melted chocolate if it is powdered.

Josh, you tested out 100 grams a day on yourself against a 20 gram known safe limit? Wow, you the man.

I see the most likely negative side effect of Ribose is hypoglycemia. What kind of glucose numbers do you normally have three hours after meals during the day, and what were those numbers on 100 grams of D-Ribose? Or do you even use a glucometer?

### persistence — 2014-03-12T06:18:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-ribose.9143/post-114957>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=103861)
>
> Has anyone tried cooking/baking with D-ribose? Is heat a problem? I know it goes well in hot coffee or tea.

nonchalant, I decided to research this the hard way. So far the only half life study for ribose I could find was this chemical analysis:

[http://www.pnas.org/content/92/18/8158.full.pdf](http://www.pnas.org/content/92/18/8158.full.pdf)

Unfortunately I am not sure if what they call "ribose" is what we buy as "D-ribose" so we should first calibrate to the right type of ribose referenced in that paper. They talk about three different forms. At roughly 212 degrees Farenheit the half life is around 73 minutes. That's not great, as most baking recipes would probably have the temperature well above that. What we really need is a study that has a fuller range of temperatures, so we can interpolate more precise values.

The other point that I saw in my reading is that Ribose creates more advanced glycation end products (AGEs) than glucose does, and that's not good news. AGEs are incredibly nasty and harmful. See for example:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3704564/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3704564/)

Based on that, I think Ribose would be best used in liquids. If I were mixing it into a chocolate / cacao, I would probably mix it in at the very end, then quickly pour into the chocolate mold and refrigerate.

I don't have enough information yet, but I probably would not use it in baking. Higher temperatures for extended periods of time mean shorter half-life, and you increase the risk of AGEs.

But, then again, I am trying to be conservative. As the study I linked above shows, AGE data on food items containing glucose are hard enough to find. The odds anyone studied D-Ribose AGE products is somewhere between zero and zero point zero one.

### Josh — 2014-03-12T07:08:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-ribose.9143/post-114958>

I had no glucose problems with it. I still routinely use 30grams many days. I use it alone or in chocolate mostly. The Christmas cake used almond flour with nuts and cacao and a little dried fruit.
One needs some sort of entity to buy wholesale from bioenergy, 10kg minimum.

### persistence — 2014-03-12T07:13:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-ribose.9143/post-114959>

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=114958)
>
> I had no glucose problems with it. I still routinely use 30grams many days. I use it alone or in chocolate mostly. The Christmas cake used almond flour with nuts and cacao and a little dried fruit.

Can you share what are your glucose numbers with and without d-ribose?

> One needs some sort of entity to buy wholesale from bioenergy, 10kg minimum.

Every corporate coffee room clearly needs 10kg of d-ribose.

# Thread: More support that says Birhtcontrol has negative effects on women's health
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-support-that-says-birhtcontrol-has-negative-effects-on-womens-health.9144/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Shijin13 — 2013-12-19T21:37:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-support-that-says-birhtcontrol-has-negative-effects-on-womens-health.9144/post-103865>

[http://www.vanityfair.com/politics/2014/01/nuvaring-lethal-contraceptive-trial](http://www.vanityfair.com/politics/2014/01/nuvaring-lethal-contraceptive-trial)

interesting for every one to read b/c it highlights what Jack's been saying about BC

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-20T00:35:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-support-that-says-birhtcontrol-has-negative-effects-on-womens-health.9144/post-103879>

The best birth control is a rubber........not drugs.

### Josh — 2013-12-20T01:55:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-support-that-says-birhtcontrol-has-negative-effects-on-womens-health.9144/post-103884>

In the early 1980's, I worked on resettling refugees from SE Asia in New York and Philadelphia. They were giving the women implants in the refugee camps before they came here. Many of them developed health issues and became sterile. When I began practicing years later, I was shocked to learn that these drugs were now approved and regularly given to women starting as early as adolescence. Of course I have seen many of the same issues in practice that the refugee women complained of in the early 1980's.

### Hope — 2013-12-20T02:03:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-support-that-says-birhtcontrol-has-negative-effects-on-womens-health.9144/post-103885>

I have heard that some of the work Bill Gates does in Africa includes giving women vaccinations of some kind that also can make them less fertile, maybe sterile. They talk of population control. Made me want to puke. Not sure if it is true, but...

### Josh — 2013-12-20T03:06:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-support-that-says-birhtcontrol-has-negative-effects-on-womens-health.9144/post-103890>

I believe what they were testing on the refugees was the early versions of Depo Provera.

### cantweight — 2013-12-20T06:30:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-support-that-says-birhtcontrol-has-negative-effects-on-womens-health.9144/post-103897>

Man, I used nuva ring for years. Everyday I see another commercial for some drug some doc prescribed me...vioxx, phen-fen, yaz. Im amazed Im still here! This sickens me. As someone with a latex allergy condoms are not an option and the lambskin ones would have put us in the poor house! Nuva ring was the only birth control my body tolerated. I cant imagine how much of the hormone shit storm I am dealing with comes from all of these things that were govt approved and considered safe. It disgusts me!

At 12 yrs of age my daughter was telling me of girlfriends on the pill to help regulate their periods....my heart breaks for these girls and the path they are being lead down at such an early age.

### Inger — 2013-12-20T10:15:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-support-that-says-birhtcontrol-has-negative-effects-on-womens-health.9144/post-103900>

So happy I have never touched any BC except rubber....... too scary to me (sometimes fear can be good ...)

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-20T14:23:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-support-that-says-birhtcontrol-has-negative-effects-on-womens-health.9144/post-103907>

I cant imagine how much of the hormone shit storm I am dealing with comes from all of these things that were govt approved and considered safe. It disgusts me!
>
> At 12 yrs of age my daughter was telling me of girlfriends on the pill to help regulate their periods....my heart breaks for these girls and the path they are being lead down at such an early age.

There are many non latex options now........and they dont cost a ton unless youre screwing 10 times a day. And if you are youre likely in pretty decent shape so dont sweat the small stuff.

### Shijin13 — 2013-12-20T15:51:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/more-support-that-says-birhtcontrol-has-negative-effects-on-womens-health.9144/post-103912>

You know what people aren't talking about is teaching and using the fertility awareness method as a method of birthcontrol - We used it for almost 3yr w/o getting pregnant. we used condoms around my fertile time period to ensure conception wouldn't occur.

I've known people who've used it their entire adult life w/o a single oops. One of my friends has been using it since the late 90's. She has two planned children.

this is one of the best books that explains the different kinds of bc - [http://amzn.to/1fLeLOv](http://amzn.to/1fLeLOv)
FAM is just as reliable if not more so that BC, when used correctly.I recommend it to any one who wants to stop taking bc. Chaos will learn this method.

# Thread: Talk to me about cold  damp fresh air
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/talk-to-me-about-cold-damp-fresh-air.9147/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### agatha — 2013-12-20T00:23:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/talk-to-me-about-cold-damp-fresh-air.9147/post-103875>

We are having a patch of damp cool weather here in the UK winter and I am feeling a tremendous urge to go outside and breathe in huge lungfuls of this lovely damp fresh air - it makes me feel so refreshed and if I have a headache it disappears instantly when I go outside. I am planning to put my tent up in the garden this weekend so that I can sleep outdoors in this air - I know that it will make me sleep very deeply.

Can anyone explain why this cool damp air is so beneficial please? The dampness seems particularly important as well as the coolness. My friends don't seem to notice how wonderful it is but for me it seems like a powerful medicine.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-20T00:28:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/talk-to-me-about-cold-damp-fresh-air.9147/post-103877>

1\. Cool= Cold Thermogenesis=Hall effect= better energy production in your cells = EE10. Simple.
2\. Damp= natural water recycled by mother Earth to re structure itself in aerosols in your lungs from the Earth natural cycles. This helps with O2 production and energy see cool above.

### Hope — 2013-12-20T02:04:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/talk-to-me-about-cold-damp-fresh-air.9147/post-103886>

Another epic. This must explain why my favorite outside temp is the 40s-50s. I thought it's bc my ancestry is Northern European...

### Hemming — 2013-12-20T10:11:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/talk-to-me-about-cold-damp-fresh-air.9147/post-103898>

Perhaps somewhat related [http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/magazine-21537988](http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/magazine-21537988) . The same goes for Denmark, infants always nap outside regardless of the weather and we have the same saying - there is no bad weather only bad clothing

### Inger — 2013-12-20T10:12:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/talk-to-me-about-cold-damp-fresh-air.9147/post-103899>

Oh yes! I LOVE cool damp air too!!!!! I loved the climate in Stavanger/Norway by the ocean. It was often very damp, and pretty cool but perfect temp for me! I feel super energized  it is just that kind of wetter here today... light rain/rainy and +4 degree C Nice!

### agatha — 2013-12-20T23:27:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/talk-to-me-about-cold-damp-fresh-air.9147/post-103936>

Yes, I think my ancestry is probably Scandinavian too given my surname and skin/hair colouring. Interesting link Hemming - those lucky, lucky babies!! I wish someone would tuck me into a fur lined pram for a lovely outdoor nap.

Most English people seem to find damp rainy weather profoundly depressing but I find myself super energised and almost euphoric in mood, especially when it's cool too. It's especially good to go into a damp forest - the smell of the damp undergrowth is beyond wonderful. I would like to understand more about what the dampness does in the lungs and how it seems to lift the mood. But why do others hate this kind of weather? How are they different I wonder?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-21T06:17:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/talk-to-me-about-cold-damp-fresh-air.9147/post-103948>

Dont worry about them.........when you are wonderful.

# Thread: Do I Want Oxidized Cells?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-i-want-oxidized-cells.9148/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-20T02:20:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-i-want-oxidized-cells.9148/post-103887>

Hello everyone, I hope my constant posts aren't getting excessive. I've been trying to understand the effects of redox potential on cell physiology and function lately and it's terribly fascinating. I've been skimming the theories of Gilbert Ling and Szent-Gyorgi (both of whom saw redox potential as chief regulators of cell function). I've been considering Dr. Kruse's idea that the cell should be more reduced, but I cannot figure out his reasoning. According to this guy here, [http://pranarupa.wordpress.com/page/2/](http://pranarupa.wordpress.com/page/2/),
"the increased CO2 is known to improve muscle relaxation, this is no doubt due to its capacity to restore the cell to healthy oxidative metabolism, resulting in decreased intracellular pressure, shifting redox balance towards a more â€œoxidizedâ€￾ state, accompanied by increased ATP synthesis, increased water structure and resulting higher level of energy. This decreased intracellular pressure in Viktor Schaubergerâ€™s terms may be a result of a â€œbiological vacuumâ€™ and a resulting inflow of â€˜ethericiesâ€™ increasing cellular energy and structure (more on potential connections between Schauberger and Peat in a later post)."
Obviously this Pranarupa person thinks that an oxidized cell state is more healthy than a reduced cell state. He also states that "The Living-state for Szent-Gyorgyi (1977) was the electronically desaturated state, that allows for the development of increasingly complex life. Szent-Gyorgyi argues that cancer results from a failure in electronic desaturation, leading to loss of differentiated structure (Pohl et al. 1977)." and that cancer occurs in a more reduced state, while metabolism of normal cells can be regenerated by creating a more oxidized environment. I did a bit more research of my own and I found that NAD+ (the oxidized form) activates the Sirtuin 1 gene, which is responsible for longevity. A high NAD+/NADH (the reduced state) activates the Sirtuin 1 gene. I also found that the ratio of NAD+/NADH falls in cells as an organism ages, suggesting that cells become reduced as they age, and lose oxidizing potential. What do you guys think? What's with the conflicting theories? Am I misunderstanding something?

### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-20T02:24:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-i-want-oxidized-cells.9148/post-103888>

Here's more info from [http://www.cell.com/abstract/S0092-8674(13)00755-1:](http://www.cell.com/abstract/S0092-8674\(13\)00755-1:) (Remember, NAD+ is oxidized)
Highlights
Enhancing NAD+ availability extends lifespan in C. elegans through sir-2.1
The NAD+/sir-2.1-dependent activation of mitochondrial UPR is crucial for longevity
Higher NAD+ bioavailability protects against ROS by enhancing daf-16 activity
UPRmt is a conserved target of the NAD+/SIRT1 axis in mammalian cells
Summary

NAD+ is an important cofactor regulating metabolic homeostasis and a rate-limiting substrate for sirtuin deacylases. We show that NAD+ levels are reduced in aged mice and Caenorhabditis elegans and that decreasing NAD+ levels results in a further reduction in worm lifespan. Conversely, genetic or pharmacological restoration of NAD+ prevents age-associated metabolic decline and promotes longevity in worms

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-20T04:25:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-i-want-oxidized-cells.9148/post-103892>

Oxidation has its role........it is a signal we use. The problem arises when the smoke signals are too faint or a pyroclastic flow......I like the fact you question things but I wonder if you think a bit too concretely.........biology and health are not concrete sciences.......but you seem to think they are........and I dont. Maybe that is your issue with my work.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-20T04:26:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/do-i-want-oxidized-cells.9148/post-103893>

Maybe if you read the Quilt.......you'd see what it says in the beginning. To get where I am headed you have to embrace paradox and enigma......that is where the truth is buried. Its not on the road in front of your eyes.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

I went from no blood pressure medication in the summer to 1 pill/day in October-November and now 2/day in December. Also had headaches come back after not having them for almost a year.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-22T00:36:29+0200

### caroline — 2013-12-22T00:54:31+0200

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-22T01:08:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/winter-solstice-reconnect-with-yourself-and-the-night.9155/post-104007>

We are each gifted in a unique and important way. It is our privilege and our adventure to discover our own special light. Tonight, the shortest day of the year tap your light. In the depths of my personal despair in the winter of 2005, I finally learned that within me there was a hidden invincible summer that could heal me. Every night we need to get back to walking in the cold dark so each of us can learn to turn on their own light.

### Shijin13 — 2013-12-22T01:40:55+0200

### Inger — 2013-12-22T11:38:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/winter-solstice-reconnect-with-yourself-and-the-night.9155/post-104035>

So beautiful.. We need to explore the dark to find our light! I love it... the darkness takes away all the noise around and I turn inside. I went for a dip in the river the other day, it was already totally dark I could barely see the stairs into the water so I had to guess and go very slow and carefully. Here is no snow either now so very dark. It was so magic to be there naked in the river in the darkness and I could see the stars! Very beautiful indeed

This is my second winter of darkness... and I will get even more healing done. I am fascinated by the power of it and what it does... I could never have thought I could be fascinated with anything like this, not in my wildest dreams...

### Huck — 2013-12-22T13:16:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/winter-solstice-reconnect-with-yourself-and-the-night.9155/post-104037>

I recently downloaded this free book from Amazon/Kindle. [As a Man Thinketh](http://www.amazon.com/As-Man-Thinketh-James-Allen-ebook/dp/B0082VKK5Y/ref=sr_1_1?s=digital-text&ie=UTF8&qid=1387710864&sr=1-1&keywords=as+a+man+thinketh), James Allen. Thr entire book reinforces the idea that Gretchen and Jack have laid out in this thread.

> Mind is the Master power that moulds and makes, And Man is Mind, and evermore he takes The tool of Thought, and, shaping what he wills, Brings forth a thousand joys, a thousand ills:â€” He thinks in secret, and it comes to pass: Environment is but his looking-glass.

> THE aphorism, "As a man thinketh in his heart so is he," not only embraces the whole of a man's being, but is so comprehensive as to reach out to every condition and circumstance of his life. A man is literally what he thinks, his character being the complete sum of all his thoughts. As the plant springs from, and could not be without, the seed, so every act of a man springs from the hidden seeds of thought, and could not have appeared without them. This applies equally to those acts called "spontaneous" and "unpremeditated" as to those, which are deliberately executed. Act is the blossom of thought, and joy and suffering are its fruits; thus does a man garner in the sweet and bitter fruitage of his own husbandry.
>
>
> "Thought in the mind hath made us, What we are By thought was wrought and built. If a man's mind Hath evil thoughts, pain comes on him as comes The wheel the ox behind.... .. If one endure In purity of thought, joy follows him As his own shadowâ€” sure."​

Allen, James (2012-05-12). As a Man Thinketh. Kindle Edition.

What wonderful things will be on your Christmas table?

It is amazing how my mindset has completely changed - all I want is seafood ... and then more seafood!

I do want cherries and peaches tho ....... and stilton cheese .... and some malbec ...

and chocolate .... but I am realising that any chocolate is dangerous territory for me. I am better off staying away from it!

but some smoked salmon and some stilton or goat cheese or brie .... and a glass of red - it doesn't get any better than that for me.......

I could easily put a straw in a bottle of port .... and go for it!

It just occurred to me ......you would be really busy because of all the people on the roads and all the alcohol? You must see some horrific things over the holidays? How do you shake it off? I would guess you never get used to it? Especially children...... Do you at least wear a Santa hat?

So - who is drinking your malbec?

# Thread: Preventing Toxic Effects of DHA?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preventing-toxic-effects-of-dha.9160/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-22T11:20:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preventing-toxic-effects-of-dha.9160/post-104033>

So, I have to say, I'm quite sold on Dr. Kruse's ideas on the importance of DHA in the brain. However, I'm also convinced by Ray Peat's arguments that DHA is toxic to mitochondria and suppresses proper mitochondrial respiration. There are studies showing that as an organism ages, it's cardiolipin, a protein present in the cellular respiration enzyme cytochrome c oxidase, becomes more unsaturated and loses the presence of saturated palmitic acid. This deterioration reduced mitochondrial respiration as can be seen in this study [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/3351630](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/3351630). Is this the choice, then? Do we have to choose between fully functional mitochondria and well structured neurons? Does anybody know of a way to prevent mitochondrial uptake of DHA and make sure it only concentrates in neurons?

### HoneyChild — 2013-12-22T12:28:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preventing-toxic-effects-of-dha.9160/post-104036>

I don't know but Jack has always said that eating DHA in its evolutionary package is better than a supplement. The two are very different. Also it's better to eat raw/undercooked seafood if possible.

### Jonathin — 2013-12-22T20:30:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preventing-toxic-effects-of-dha.9160/post-104056>

Seems like horrible research. A diet of purified casein, corn starch, sugar and corn or sardine oil . . . please! Is this science?
This is troubling but not because it indicts DHA of malfeasance.

### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-22T20:58:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preventing-toxic-effects-of-dha.9160/post-104057>

Good point Jonathan. However, even on such a poisonous diet, the animals' mitochondria fared better on the DHA free diet than the one with DHA. It still seems to suggest potential damage to mitochondria.

### nonchalant — 2013-12-22T21:49:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preventing-toxic-effects-of-dha.9160/post-104061>

Dr. Kruse has said that when we eat seafood, the DHA is reserved for the brain. DHA isn't normally used for fuel.

### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-22T22:18:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preventing-toxic-effects-of-dha.9160/post-104066>

This study seems to show the DHA being incorporate into the cardiolipin, not used for energy. Maybe the iodine prevents the cardiolipin from taking up DHA? I wish we had studies on that.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-22T22:22:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preventing-toxic-effects-of-dha.9160/post-104067>

This thread is really problematic for me. Citing bad science and Peat all in one invokes shades of why I had to ban BigPoppaChakra. The scent is discernible, like rancid fish oil in this diet. Jonathin is spot on. I'm wondering if we have a new name and the same old nonsense.

### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-22T23:14:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preventing-toxic-effects-of-dha.9160/post-104071>

Sorry Dr. Kruse, I was just trying to make sense of all the conflicting theories. Big Papa got banned? That's too bad. Peat seems to provide at least some pretty valid thoughts and I was trying to synthesize his thoughts on fish oil with yours; a quite difficult goal.

### nonchalant — 2013-12-22T23:19:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preventing-toxic-effects-of-dha.9160/post-104072>

It would be difficult to coordinate Dr. Kruse's ideas with others, because others do not have his insight into our true nature. How does Peat feel about the fact that our bodies work in a quantum fashion, as well as having backup chemical energy sources?

### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-22T23:21:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preventing-toxic-effects-of-dha.9160/post-104073>

Based on your comment about the rancid fish oil, I assume you think that unoxidized, stable DHA would not have these suppressive effects on the cardiolipin? Would it not be taken up, or would it be taken up and just not reduce the cellular respiration. If you could point me to studies on DHA's true effect on cellular respiration, I would very much appreciate it. I was really confused when Peat stated that DHA supresses mitochondrial function considering your well supported evidence that the most metabolically active tissue, the brain, also has the most DHA in it.

### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-22T23:27:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preventing-toxic-effects-of-dha.9160/post-104076>

Ray Peat does support Gilbert Ling's ideas of Association Induction as opposed to the cell-membrane, using structured water and what not. He believes that sugar burning is a better way to stabilize the coherent structure of the cell, but that's a whole different discussion! xD I'm thoroughly convinced that one of the chief effectors of health is mitochondrial respiration so I'm trying to get to the bottom of what increases mitochondrial respiration and what harms it. When I read Peat's ideas on fish oil, I didn't want to believe that DHA harmed mitochondria and I figured no one could refute his DHA thoughts better than the people at this forum!

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-23T00:58:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preventing-toxic-effects-of-dha.9160/post-104087>

Fish oil does not and never will equal food with DHA. Peat does not understand that and never has.

### RickD — 2013-12-23T02:38:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/preventing-toxic-effects-of-dha.9160/post-104100>

To follow up on Jack's Statement above when fish oil is processed it changes from a triglyceride to an ester so it's pretty likely your body doesn't look at it the same. Now you can buy Fish oil that is changed back to a trig but it's pretty pricey stuff. Go have a look a Jack's fish oil blog and read the Science article at the end.

[http://www.jackkruse.com/is-fish-oil-good-or-bad/](http://www.jackkruse.com/is-fish-oil-good-or-bad/)

The article says in mice fish oil is less effective in lowering blood pressure likely due to the Trig vs Ester issue.

A few years back I was pumping the fish oil because it was convenient vs sourcing wild AK salmon and after about 6 months I went through a couple week period where my nose would start bleed while out running and another instance riding up a chairlift. Dumped the fish oil soon afterwards and went through the hassle to get Copper River Sockeye from AK. As a former Alaskan these are the best sockeyes out there because the Copper River which flows off the Copper glacier 300 miles inland and runs upto 12mph meaning only the stoutest fish can make the journey up to their breeding grounds.

Used to be more pricey, but in 2013 I noticed Costco stores across the Pacific Northwest and even Alberta were carrying it. US Price was $9.99/lb while in Canada it was $17.99/lb. Eating a reasonable portion 3x a week for my wife and I works out to about 120 lbs/yr. Not cheap but not ridiculous either.

# Thread: Cookbook for Wisconsin?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cookbook-for-wisconsin.9163/>
**Subforum:** Community Cookbook
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### meihao01 — 2013-12-23T04:33:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cookbook-for-wisconsin.9163/post-104103>

I moved to Wisconsin for college from Asia last summer. Since I love to cook, I did a little research in order to find a cookbook. If I get an Asian cookbook, most of the ingredients would be hard to find. I guess a typical American cookbook would work, but pizzas, burgers, or sandwiches are already everywhere. Italian and Mexican food are similar and the ingredients won't be hard to find. However, the cuisines seem hard to cook. Finally, I chose a German cookbook. I thought the ingredients, like fresh cream and cheese, would be easy to find and eastern European cuisines are usually simple. The problem is that the cookbook seems to assume that I leave in a small European town with a farmer's market open every morning. I can't find fresh cream, kohlrabi, duck, rabbit, or other important ingredients. I got totally confused. These things are not supposed to be hard to find in an agricultural state like Wisconsin. I feel like everything is pre-made and because of that, some ingredients are not on the shelf for the they can't be pre-made. Any recommendation for cookbook?

### caroline — 2013-12-23T06:28:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cookbook-for-wisconsin.9163/post-104109>

Dr. Kruse has a cookbook ..... go to the home page...

Welcome!

There is a good foodie site .....nom nom paleo....and lots of others. Enjoy!

### nicld — 2013-12-23T16:51:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cookbook-for-wisconsin.9163/post-104119>

I agree with Caroline, check out the Kruse cookbook. If you go to Amazon and search for Wisconsin cookbooks you will find a bunch there. There is nothing really special about the foods in WI except for the cheese and cranberries.

Are you going to school at Madison?

I have lived here my whole life and have never had rabbit or squirrel but venison I get every year but you have to know someone who has some since i don't think it is sold in stores.

Nicole

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-23T18:01:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cookbook-for-wisconsin.9163/post-104126>

I do not think Wisconsin has good food at all

# Thread: What biochemical changes occur that facilitate fat breakdown?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-biochemical-changes-occur-that-facilitate-fat-breakdown.9164/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-23T11:03:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-biochemical-changes-occur-that-facilitate-fat-breakdown.9164/post-104110>

Pretty simple question that I, for some reason, cannot seem to find. I'm sure someone here will be able to help me out. What biochemical adaptation actually induces lipolysis/free fatty acid release? Free fatty acids are released into your blood and these are used as fuel for ketosis. But how exactly does the body initiate release of the free fatty acids? I know hormone-sensitive lipase is involved in there somewhere but, as the name suggests, there is a hormonal change that the lipase is sensitive to. What is it?

### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-23T11:12:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-biochemical-changes-occur-that-facilitate-fat-breakdown.9164/post-104111>

I should have specified. I'm interested in how the body releases free fatty acids while in ketosis. Not just normal fat breakdown. I'm not sure if the hormonal cascade is any different.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-23T17:57:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-biochemical-changes-occur-that-facilitate-fat-breakdown.9164/post-104123>

read CT 6. it is all laid out there

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-23T17:57:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-biochemical-changes-occur-that-facilitate-fat-breakdown.9164/post-104124>

and the cascade he is quite different

### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-23T20:35:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-biochemical-changes-occur-that-facilitate-fat-breakdown.9164/post-104135>

Thanks Dr. Kruse, I'll check out CT6!

### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-23T22:16:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-biochemical-changes-occur-that-facilitate-fat-breakdown.9164/post-104152>

Very informative read but it didn't seem to cover one thing that I'm looking for. According to Peter over at hyperlipid [http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com/2009/11/brief-discussion-of-ketosis.html?m=1](http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com/2009/11/brief-discussion-of-ketosis.html?m=1), the lipolysis of ketosis is increased due to increased levels of adrenaline and cortisol. I thought we wanted to minimize our stress hormones but here, Peter is telling us that the hormonal changes necessary to drive lipolysis in ketosis are a rising of stress hormones like adrenaline and cortisol? Is there a way that ketotic lipolysis can be initiated without jacking up your stress hormones?

# Thread: you have health insurance; did you know that no one in your area accepts it????

### Shijin13 — 2013-12-23T19:24:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-have-health-insurance-did-you-know-that-no-one-in-your-area-accepts-it.9170/post-104131>

for those who are excited about having access to health insurance for 2013 - guess what? you may not have access to doctors or hospitals who take it.

Choas had a PEd Endo apt this Thrusday but it was cancled . Now Georgetown Hospital Pediatrics/Medstar is trying to figure out how to get us in in this week or next week so Choas can been seen and still have her visit covered by our current ins provider United Health Care b/c when I told her we changed health ins and that GT/Medstar wasn't accept BCBS (or BCBS Federal) for next year she was aghast, put me on hold and verified w/ their ins section. When she came back on - she was shocked that GT/Medstar will only be accepting one BlueCross ins next year - which is Anthem BC. Welcome to the new age of health care, courtesy of the ACA. Buckle up folks it's going to get worse, as you may now have hospitals, and physicians in your area - who can't treat you. How about them apples.....

### fitness@home — 2013-12-23T20:37:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-have-health-insurance-did-you-know-that-no-one-in-your-area-accepts-it.9170/post-104136>

Those apples are rotten!

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-have-health-insurance-did-you-know-that-no-one-in-your-area-accepts-it.9170/post-104138>

# Thread: Merry Christmas Everyone!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/merry-christmas-everyone.9174/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 24
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2013-12-23T23:08:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/merry-christmas-everyone.9174/post-104156>

It is Christmas Eve here in OZ .......

I wish so many wonderful things for all of us this coming year...Peace, joy, love, contentment - but most of all - a fire in our belly to live our most authentic life.

Merry Christmas and Happy Holidays to everyone here.

Merry Christmas Dr. K.
Merry Christmas Misty and all your staff that make all this happen!

# Thread: Sugar vs. Fat Oxidation with Respect to Carbon Dioxide
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar-vs-fat-oxidation-with-respect-to-carbon-dioxide.9184/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-26T10:12:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar-vs-fat-oxidation-with-respect-to-carbon-dioxide.9184/post-104249>

To start this post, I'll list some of the numerous benefits associated with carbon dioxide. It's a powerful vasodilator, it promotes energy consumption and boosts the metabolism, it stimulates mitochondria bio-genesis, it enhances body oxygenation (this is called the Bohr effect), it protects neurons, it's a muscle relaxant, and it has many other desirable effects on the body. The beneficial effects of carbon dioxide are outlined in quite a detailed way at this site, here: [http://www.normalbreathing.com/](http://www.normalbreathing.com/)

Further, Dr. Kruse has mentioned the beneficial aspects of CO2 before. Here is what he wrote in a previous thread that I started a few weeks ago:

"Under the control of insulin, glucose adsorption sites on proteins and enzymes become exposed to the environment, and glucose can be burned to replenish the ATP or shunned if the cell is made IR as mentioned above in this thread. When glucose is the fuel, at the same time, carbon dioxide is produced, which functions to stabilize the resting living state. Here is where my cosmology links come back for you to consider........CO2 in cold causes matter in galaxies to condense under the force of the electromagnetic and gravitational forces of physics. In your body the same thing happens in the ABSENCE OF CYTOKINES OR INFLAMMATION. This effect is called the Bohr effect, increases the efficiency with which oxygen is unloaded from the red blood cells, curtailing the production of lactate and protons. Protons are positively charged and consider acids and they do not like Oxygen. In this highly energized coherent state, potassium is preferentially adsorbed to the ionized carboxylate groups on intracellular proteins. This satisfactorily explains why the administration of glucose and insulin can uncomfortably lower blood potassium levels............this is has huge implications for explaining rigor mortis."

Now, I don't fully understand the complex biochemistry behind this post but I got one thing. CO2 is good stuff. My concern can be seen in this very comment by Dr. Kruse though. He specifies that this beneficial CO2 is produced "when glucose is the fuel". This concept is used by a variety of anti-ketosis gurus to justify sugar burning. Fructose and glucose oxidation produces significantly more CO2 per unit of oxygen than fat burning. They say that this boost of "coherent state" promoting CO2 is what makes sugar burning more advantageous to health than fat burning. Now, I'm quite convinced that ketosis/fat-adaptation is the preferred metabolic state, but I can't quite seem to refute the idea that sugar burning promotes healing CO2, an idea which has seemed to be validated by Dr. Kruse himself.

What do you guys think of this? Are the numerous advantages of ketosis merely more important than this one drawback? Or does ketosis have a hidden source of CO2?
Thanks.

### HoneyChild — 2013-12-26T12:51:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar-vs-fat-oxidation-with-respect-to-carbon-dioxide.9184/post-104250>

All I know is that the liver can make glucose via gluconeogenesis so it's not as though we should be aiming to have a blood glucose reading of zero.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-26T19:44:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar-vs-fat-oxidation-with-respect-to-carbon-dioxide.9184/post-104260>

boom........................

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-26T20:00:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar-vs-fat-oxidation-with-respect-to-carbon-dioxide.9184/post-104263>

People with gastric bypass present data that show you obesity is not a metabolic disease but a quantum one of an altered mitochondria to tunneling of electrons and alteration in how positively charge protons are handled.

Read a bit and mull over what Peter is knocking on?

[http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com/2013/12/protons-32-post-obese-insulin-induced.html](http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com/2013/12/protons-32-post-obese-insulin-induced.html)

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-26T20:04:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar-vs-fat-oxidation-with-respect-to-carbon-dioxide.9184/post-104264>

Then let me mind screw you with where I am going.........on the very same issue.

Consider astrophysics for a minute: The only difference is the scales.....the process of what happens in space and in our mitochondria are congruent.

Plasmas are the phase of matter where atoms are ripped apart into electrons and nuclei. Plasmas are primed for strong electromagnetic force interactions with the world around them, because of all the "loose stripped charged particles" they contain. So what contains them in our mitochondria? Mitochondria allow for plasma like interactions in the quantum cell because electrons and protons from foods are stripped and charges are developed. What controls their actions once made?

Hotter plasmas often have nearly all of their atoms broken apart and ionized, whereas cooler plasmas may be only partly ionized. Cold organizes plasma’s. It turns out that magnetic fields also control plasma’s better than anything we know about in astrophysics. In nuclear reactors we can use a magnetic field to control and an out of control plasma. what would happen if the magnetic field changes around a plasma of charged particles? Change the scale from a nebula star cluster and consider the human mitochondria for a moment. The native magnetic field seems to contain action in our mitochondria. But the more charged ions you have, the more electromagnetic force interactions occur within the plasma because of all the free charge, and this is what makes plasmas behave differently from non-ionized gases. Maybe this is why all mammals effectively separate electrons from protons from foods and create free heat? It seems this is the ideal way to create negative entropy by returning entropy back to the environment huh?

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-26T20:07:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar-vs-fat-oxidation-with-respect-to-carbon-dioxide.9184/post-104265>

Hey did you know CO2 is a charged molecule too? DID you know that gas plasma use CO2 and CO to condense things in cold and form a galaxy or star..........BOOM.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-26T20:11:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar-vs-fat-oxidation-with-respect-to-carbon-dioxide.9184/post-104266>

A slowing of electron transfer is the most likely outcome of any mismatch between mitochondrial and nuclear genomes. The reason relates to the mechanism of electron transfer due to poor ATP production. Quantum time is off if electron chain transport is off.

DOUBLE BOOM

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-26T20:12:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar-vs-fat-oxidation-with-respect-to-carbon-dioxide.9184/post-104267>

When ECT transport is slow, we make more free oxygen radicals from transition metals. This causes us to rely on glucose metabolism and AMPK pathways. AMPK is tied to carb fuels. NOT GOOD for life....long term unless you are believer of the Peat way.

TRIPLE BOOM

### Jonathin — 2013-12-26T20:23:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar-vs-fat-oxidation-with-respect-to-carbon-dioxide.9184/post-104268>

So we should consume glucose or carbs to get more carbon dioxide? Seem to me that the body regulates CO2 and bicarbonate ion concentration in the blood just like everything else. This is accomplished by proper neural and hormonal regulation. I think we know that piling on carbs especially in the winter does not result does not enhance biochemical or functional balance.

### QiGuy1997 — 2013-12-26T20:35:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sugar-vs-fat-oxidation-with-respect-to-carbon-dioxide.9184/post-104269>

Hmm. Forgive me if I'm misunderstanding (I probably am), but your posts seem to address the mitochondrial benefits of CO2. This seems to suggest carb burning over fat burning, as carbs produce far more CO2. This probabky isn't what you're saying but the conclusion from this seems to be boost CO2 production by burning sugar. Is there a way to extract comparable amounts of CO2 from fat/ketones as sugar burners do from glucose/fructose? I havent found any way, but you usually have radical ideas. I've been practicing Buteyko breathing in an effort to up my CO2 blood levels, but I'm concerned that ketosis will offset this benefit. By the way, I got your book for Christmas and I'm loving it.

# Thread: Question for Jack
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-for-jack.9185/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### heathercstevens — 2013-12-26T16:17:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-for-jack.9185/post-104252>

Hello there,

What do you recommend for a recurrent Ovarian cancer patient, who does not need to lose weight. She is receiving chronomodulated chemotherapy and takes a wide assortment of nutriceuticals supplements. I am wondering if there any thing she can do to get her body to recognize the cancer cells and attack them as they normally should, but clearly are not?

Heather

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-26T19:43:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-for-jack.9185/post-104259>

1\. I can not treat anyone on the internet. you have to rely on your doctor for advice but there are things you can do to educate yourself to ask them better questions
2\. have a look here: [http://www.jackkruse.com/what-to-do-with-new-cancer-diagnosis/](http://www.jackkruse.com/what-to-do-with-new-cancer-diagnosis/)
3\. I can show you where to look and who on this site you may try to contact who also has a similar issue. [http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?8186-Ovarian-Cancer-amp-Epi-Paleo/page2](http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?8186-Ovarian-Cancer-amp-Epi-Paleo/page2)
4\. Use of curcumin is huge.....and tumeric and black pepper on everything she eats is big. Lots of 99% dark chocolate helps the immune system..
5\. CT protocol is something to look into for her too.
6\. She needs to understand that contact with the Earth should be the goal from here on out. Grounding and "eating sunlight"
7\. Strict avoidance oif high glycemic carbs is a well founded idea in the literature with epithelial cancers like ovarian cancer
8\. Epithelial cancers tend to walk hand and hand with low Vitamin D levels, Altered cortisol and melatonin levels because of artifical light. Brain Gut 11 blog post would be one you should read.
9\. The best way to help fight cancers is to augment the patients immune system...............Read the anticancer mind. The diet advocated in it is not something I call ideal but the book is loaded with gems.

# Thread: Anti Cancer Brew
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-cancer-brew.9200/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ricardo — 2013-12-28T18:15:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-cancer-brew.9200/post-104350>

A local scientist involved in cancer research has documented, charted, the synergy produced by a tea of simple ingredients.

He uses a 3 ingredient formula consisting of:

A green tea with a high ECGC content: Sencha is a good choice.
Turmeric spice
A tiny amount of black pepper

The ingredients are combined and added to boiling water then simmered for 10 minutes.

His chart shows that the resultant molecule is quite a bit stronger than if the ingredients were taken individually.

I don't know how to explain how this works. However, Dr Beliveau gave a seminar at the Jewish General Hospital, a well known cancer research center. A friend who is a surgeon attended and was impressed with the science behind this simple formula.

Dr Beliveau's "CV" and a word about his research:

[http://www.mcgill.ca/translational-research-cancer/researcher-biographies/beliveau](http://www.mcgill.ca/translational-research-cancer/researcher-biographies/beliveau)

It does not taste good: however, I take it as a medicine.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-28T19:10:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-cancer-brew.9200/post-104354>

I think this is a great cocktail for cancer.

### nonchalant — 2013-12-30T01:16:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-cancer-brew.9200/post-104412>

Turmeric tea tastes wonderful. This recipe could have green tea added to it:

[http://www.marksdailyapple.com/creamy-turmeric-tea/#axzz2ouOrBANp](http://www.marksdailyapple.com/creamy-turmeric-tea/#axzz2ouOrBANp)

### Ricardo — 2013-12-30T06:15:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-cancer-brew.9200/post-104428>

I inquired about adding ingredients and was told that the best effect was to follow the formula without making any changes (or adding anything else no matter if its a fine ingredient. For instance, I had suggested cayenne. )

In addition, there should be a minimum of a 10 minutes of simmering as recommended. Despite that bit it tastes bitter ( the green tea tastes bitter after 3 or 4 minutes of brewing ) there are many cancer patients who take it daily.

Your recipe sounds delicious ( its all good) and I am not meaning to sound harsh. Its just that it is considered a form of medicine and there is a preferred formula that has been documented by Dr Beliveau who holds the Chair for Cancer Prevention and Treatment at the University of Quebec.

I want to make that clear in case there is someone who needs results for themselves or for someone they care about. Cancer is so widespread in these times.

I was informed that its best to follow the formula to the letter. I hope this helps.

PS: And thank you to Dr Kruse for weighing in.

### nonchalant — 2013-12-30T18:41:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-cancer-brew.9200/post-104453>

> [Ricardo said:](/goto/post?id=104428)
>
> I inquired about adding ingredients and was told that the best effect was to follow the formula without making any changes (or adding anything else no matter if its a fine ingredient. For instance, I had suggested cayenne. )

No worries, Ricardo. If I had cancer, I would be very careful about adding additional ingredients also, especially butter or almond milk.
Turmeric and black pepper have a history of working well together, and green tea is well known to help us control our cells that have turned cancerous for any reason. Thanks for bringing this combination to our attention.

### QuantumRadical — 2014-01-02T00:23:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-cancer-brew.9200/post-104570>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=104453)
>
> No worries, Ricardo. If I had cancer, I would be very careful about adding additional ingredients also, especially butter or almond milk.
> Turmeric and black pepper have a history of working well together, and green tea is well known to help us control our cells that have turned cancerous for any reason. Thanks for bringing this combination to our attention.

is there a recommended distribution of the three ingredients for the tea, turmeric, and pepper? In teaspoons? Or just adjust to taste?

### Ricardo — 2014-01-02T03:11:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anti-cancer-brew.9200/post-104576>

> [QuantumRadical said:](/goto/post?id=104570)
>
> is there a recommended distribution of the three ingredients for the tea, turmeric, and pepper? In teaspoons? Or just adjust to taste?

About a level teaspoon of both a green tea with a high ECGC content ( Sencha for instance) and turmeric powder. Add a tiny pinch of black pepper. It should steep a full ten minutes.

Some prefer to use turmeric root that has been crushed in a garlic press. Turmeric stains so that way can be a bit messy. Same quantity, about a teaspoon.

Here in Quebec, there are researchers doing work in neutraceuticals, especially nutritional strategies to prevent cancer. I am hoping to hear of more findings and will post any that could be helpful.

Thank you!

Happy New Year to you too!

# Thread: Finally got here...

**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Andrea — 2013-12-28T18:58:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-here.9201/post-104351>

Hi all,

I accidentally started a [Journal](http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?9116-Rerun-here-I-am-and-now-what&p=103591) rather than posting my introduction here... I hope to get some comments and advice there too, though.

Anyway, I live in the darkness of northern Europe and have been doing Leptin reset for a bunch of weeks. Started with CT recently and have just started taking cold showers with the coldest water my tap will give me (15-16Â°C / 60F). Unfortunately, I have no bathtub so the shower will have to do for now.

Due to my thyroid issues I've been feeling cold most of the time for several years, and have been wearing warm sweaters in the evening, so it's a huge change and surprise that I actually feel warm after a cold shower. If I didn't have any neighbors I'd probably walk around naked for an hour or two afterwards...

I've noticed that taking a cold shower soon after a meal tends to give me diarrhea - is that normal? If I wait for an hour or two after my meal, I'm fine.

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-28T19:09:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-here.9201/post-104352>

The loose stools tell you your have a circadian mismatch in the incretin gut hormones. Just keep at the reset and it will re yoke. Read CT 7 to learn more about the gut hormones like ghrelin and agouti or search them on the site search function

### Jack Kruse — 2013-12-28T19:09:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-here.9201/post-104353>

Oh........and welcome. I am in Europe right now as we speak.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/observation-on-dawning-phenomenon.9207/post-104397>

I am 53 yr old T2D/Hashi's following the Ancient pathway for two and a half years.For the past two months or so I have been observing the following:
As I wake in the am about 5:30 I have a hot flash accompanied by light perspiration over my entire body and the disappearance of morning chubby. I mean gone. I test BG upon rising and it is usually quite high (135-190) The high fasting BG is nothing new nor is losing chubby to elevated BG, but the hot flash/sweating thing is new. This has been regular. I sleep well and do not regularly sweat at night. I believe this is due to cortisol secretion. What might be changing? Any thoughts?

### Albert83BCN — 2013-12-30T14:50:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/carbs.9213/post-104444>

Ketosis is not just a black or white state, it can be further enhanced, and to reap its benefits you want it to be deep, that is measured in the ketone bodies in blood in general (but specially the beta-hydroxybutyrate ketone that fuels the brain) and you can be at a decent point limiting to 50 gr of low GI carbs according to the common low-carb community consensus, but to reach a deep ketosis state of near 3mmol that seems to be the optimal I think it will requiere a little bit less or not at all, and also an undercaloric diet and not much protein also. Some aminoacids alter ketosis in different ways also.

You can also not be in ketosis at all and still benefit from the 'lower carb' diet in comparison with the SAD or others, it depends on what you're after and your exact context.

### caroline — 2014-02-01T08:28:52+0200

> The researchers found that participants exposed to blue light consistently rated themselves as less sleepy, had quicker reaction times and fewer lapses of attention during the performance tests compared to those who were exposed to green light. They also showed changes in brain activity patterns that indicated a more alert state.
>
> "These results contribute to our understanding of how light impacts the brain and open up a new range of possibilities for using light to improve human alertness, productivity and safety," explained Steven Lockley, PhD, neuroscientist at BWH and senior investigator of the study. "While helping to improve alertness in night workers has obvious safety benefits, day shift workers may also benefit from better quality lighting that would not only help them see better but also make them more alert."

# Thread: Chrysanthemama's Journaling Journey
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chrysanthemamas-journaling-journey.9218/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 65
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Chrysanthemama — 2013-12-30T20:59:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chrysanthemamas-journaling-journey.9218/post-104463>

I woke early this morning after 8 hours of excellent sleep. After my morning affirmations and gratitudes, I suddenly had the idea that I needed to begin a journal here. I believe it will be helpful for me as I start fresh in 2014, and as a way to be held accountable. I'll provide a little background, and I hope to write here weekly throughout the next year and beyond.

\~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
How in the heck did I find this place?

Back in 2011, I found that my weight had soared despite the fact that I am incredibly active. I have always been stocky and muscular, but I was becoming just plain fat. Despite doing martial arts several times a week, lifting weights, cycling, trail running, and hiking, I was carrying around quite a load of fat on top of all that muscle. A few years prior to 2011, I ditched bread and tortillas from my diet. I substituted one meal a day with soup, took up spinning, and lost 17 pounds. That was remarkable! I was in my late 30s and had begun to feel what I assumed was a slowing of my metabolism. As time went by, I found myself adding a piece or two of garlic bread to my plate, and ended up eating the bun instead of avoiding it. And that's where I found myself in 2011. Fat. Gross. Embarrassed. I was an athlete, after all! Why was I the one who looked pudgy when all my other athlete friends looked amazing?

I have been an herbalist for over 20 years. Trained in Traditional Chinese Medicine, Ayurveda, and western herbal medicine traditions, I have long known and practiced healing through nutrition. My husband and I have always eaten fresh, whole foods, and with the exception of crackers, pasta and tortillas (We live in New Mexico!), we didn't eat many processed foods. We did, however, consume quite a lot of legumes. Traditional New Mexican food consists of a lot of beans, and we loved making dal. My biggest grain-based consumption, however, has long been beer. Micro-brewed, fresh, hoppy ale. I blame that on going to college in the Pacific Northwest at a time when brew pubs were becoming all the rage. But in general we ate good. We avoided fast food like the plague, ate a shit ton of greens and veggies, and loved to make stir fry. Because my husband and his father are bow hunters, we've long been blessed with fresh wild game, and I grew up eating home-grown eggs, chickens, and raw milk.

But by 2011, I knew that something was going on. I felt out of control. I had started experiencing swelling in my left ankle and I began to freak out that maybe I had some form of pre-diabetes, which I thought was plainly absurd! As an herbalist, I am a member of the American Herbalist's Guild, the only licensing body for herbal professionals in the country. As part of their email list, I received all kinds of information on just about anything nutrition, herbal, or health. One day, an herbalist named Karen Vaughn posted a link to Jack's website. She was responding to a post about diabetes from another member, and it piqued my interest. She was claiming he was reversing diabetes and other AI conditions. I clicked on over to Jack's site and proceeded to have my mind blown.

From my first moments browsing through the Quilt, reading about CT, and the Leptin Reset, I was hooked. But skeptical. I would read a little, think about it, wonder about it, dismiss it, and then come back for more. CT completely blew me away. "Why should losing weight be so contrived," I thought. I must've said it 50 times a day. "Grain-free? Not for me, how in the hell can I give up beer for 6 weeks?" I worked through all these questions and thoughts and emotions for months. I continued to read and wonder and have my mind blown again and again. I didn't understand everything, but I was curious to learn more.

\~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
Paleo? Can I do it?

Back in April 2012, I bought a book that Jack had referred to on the Quilt, "The Monk Who Sold his Ferarri," by Robin Sharma. It is an awesome book, similar in a way to "The Alchemist," in that it is a fable meant to convey some pretty deep stuff. This book reminded me of my own intimate relationship with meditation, awareness, and power, all of which seemed to have fallen by the wayside for the past several years. During this same time (end of 2011 and beginning of 2012) I had become at risk for losing my job. In an industry that had seemed bullet-proof for years, we suddenly had no funding. A political exercise became the demise of many people's jobs. I was freaked, because I am the breadwinner for my family. With two daughters and a musician husband, a beautiful home we built ourselves, and plenty of debt, the thought of losing my job with nothing viable on the future was complete madness for me. I was stressed out.

After reading the book, I began practicing meditation again regularly. I also started journaling again--something that I did religiously for decades, until my house burned down and I lost my lifetime worth of writing--poetry, fiction, and stacks of journals. The building where I work is an old middle school, and just happens to be situated on the most beautiful canyon. I started finding myself drawn to edge of the canyon several times each week where I would meditate, write, and observe. Sometimes I found myself wracked with emotion, bawling my eyes out as I would write my thoughts down on paper. Within a week of reading the book, being drawn to writing, realizing that I need to figure out what the hell I am going to do with my life, setting a web of intention throughout my workspace that I could not be laid off, and meditation, I decided to try out paleo. I figured, if nothing else, I'd just try it out for 30 days. Because beer was such a part of my evening habit, I had to find a replacement for the end of the day unwinding. I bought some Zevia soda, and vowed to give it all a shot.

Within the first week, I'd lost 7 pounds. If it hadn't happened to me, I would NOT have believed it. I ended that week drinking a few beers on Friday, and doing the Warrior Dash on Saturday. I had started my period that morning, and I felt remarkably bad--foggy head, headache, and bloated belly. I drove over 2.5 hours to do a silly 5K that I hadn't even trained for. It was kind of fun, though, and I enjoyed the drive and solitude. After the race I never redeemed by free beer ticket, and came home and drank Zevia instead of beer. That was a first. By the end of the month I'd lost 12 pounds, and by the end of four months I'd lost 30 pounds. I was resolute and motivated.

### Chrysanthemama — 2013-12-30T21:00:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chrysanthemamas-journaling-journey.9218/post-104464>

And then came…
\~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
The Crash

By November 2012, I was down 35 pounds. But I started feeling awful. I was a hormonal wreck. I was eating high-fat, very low carb, and I had been feeling exceptional all summer and fall. I discovered that I no longer bonked on long bike rides and runs, and that I had gained a tremendous amount of speed during my martial arts workouts. Because I live in NM, it is not always easy for me to eat a lot of seafood, but I was trying to add in as much as possible through sardines, scallops, salmon, and shrimp. Thanksgiving came and went and I suddenly felt like I was on an emotional roller coaster. I realized that I hadn't been grounding in weeks, since early snow had seemingly shut me down from my weekly meditation/grounding sessions on the canyon. I also hadn't been doing any CT, other than being outside in the cold. I quickly changed course and made intentional efforts to ground regularly and starting CT in my bath tub at home.

Shortly before Christmas 2012 I started feeling better. I saw that Jack would be coming to New Mexico for a cold-weather MovNat workshop, and as a holiday bonus check floated through my bank account, I was excited to sign up. I began CTing in earnest in order to be prepared for the workshop. As the workshop date came closer, we just kept getting pounded by snow and storms. It was awesome. And then the magical date arrived. I'll save the story about the workshop for another entry. The most important thing I learned, though, was that nearly everything Erwan spoke about during the workshop I already knew. Many of the things we did, I've been doing forever (not the tree climbing, or certain specific MovNat skills though!!). Throughout the workshop I realized that the most important thing I was gaining was recognition of my own expertise.

That was huge.

\~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
Fast-forward to the present.

For me, 2012 was the year of transition. At the start of 2013, I stated that 2013 would be the year of action.

In many way I have been correct, although sometimes it takes longer than I want for certain things to take place.

I have spent much of the year delving deeply into building a new business. I have spent countless hours learning, reading, understanding, comprehending, dreaming, idealizing, brainstorming, and thinking, thinking, thinking. As much as I wanted things in place, I am flowing through the process. Along the way, I have found myself caught up in my own bad habits, frustrations, and challenges. This all began with painting my daughters' rooms. But really, I shouldn't need an excuse to fall backwards. I just did. It started with beer while painting. A perfectly logical marriage in my own mind. Then beer on the weekends. Then beer while camping. On vacation. Even at AHS13!! Holy smokes! That was like the epitome of pure paleo blasphemy.

And then my pants started getting tighter. And I could see the puffiness in my face. And I have been struggling to come to peace with the fact that I truly should not be drinking beer. Ever. But I don't like wine, unless it's expensive. And I am burned out on soda. And tequila is good maybe once a week, if that. And my willpower sometimes sucks, and it seems like it has sucked quite a lot for the past several months. But I am aware of this penchant for good beer, and that is half the battle, right?

So, to conclude for now, I've decided to do a 30-day full epi-paleo reset beginning on January 1st. Along with it, I am vowing to meditate at least 15 minutes each day. In addition to my daily gratitudes, I am going to add in affirmations. In addition to the normal no grains, legumes, or sugar, I will be eliminating alcohol and dairy. I need to find another fat source as coconut oil does not agree with me, but I am planning to order some duck fat and some grass fed tallow. I have never gone dairy-free, but I'm willing to give it a shot, too.

It is time for me to commit to this sort of challenge. I must take the reins of my life and continue to move forward. I know that until I am where I need to be in a whole and complete manner, I will not be moving forward at the rate that I would like. So, therefore, I am hoping to be held accountable for completing the 30-day challenge, and then for not accepting beer as a habit in my life any longer.

Happy New Year! Blessings to All.

### Chrysanthemama — 2014-01-01T01:08:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chrysanthemamas-journaling-journey.9218/post-104535>

I have been off work since last Tuesday. My husband was in Denver performing music Thursday through yesterday. I have not taken a hike since last Sunday, and boy was I desperate for a hike and my icy cold soaking spring today. It didn't happen, however, and I am trying to not let it interfere with my positive mental attitude (PMA!). Tomorrow, I don't care if it's 6am and freezing cold, I'm taking my dog on a run to my spring, and I am going to sit in there as long as I possibly can. The grounding, the CT, the energy of the canyon is what my spirit and my body are in need of. My emotions have been on high for the past couple days. I have been having the most intense shamanic-inspired dreams for weeks, my own future seems tossed up in the cosmos, I am diligently working towards the goal of launching classes and workshops for pregnant women this coming spring, I am struggling with entrenched demons, and the New Year seems to be enhancing everything to the umpteenth degree. I need to sit in cold water, in the ground, in a canyon. But I will do it tomorrow. To ring in the New Year.

Happy New Year to All! May Blessings of good fortune, health, and prosperity enrich your life in 2014 and beyond!

### Chrysanthemama — 2014-01-02T02:01:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chrysanthemamas-journaling-journey.9218/post-104575>

I slept poorly last night, the first time in a while. I woke at 3:20, approximately the time of the new moon. I could not go back to sleep after that, which is unusual. At 4:15 I went and got the dog from my daughter's room so I could take her on a hike first thing. I still could not go back to sleep, so I tried meditating for a while. Eventually I achieved a state of deep rest, but I never really fell back to sleep. In the morning, my resolve is super strong. I see the changes I need to do each day, and I vow to follow through. This morning, my resolve to follow through with a full 30-day commitment to no alcohol or dairy felt absolute.

At 7:30, I decided I'd better go before anyone else woke up. There was no one on the roads this morning, and I drove to one of my favorite trail heads. I did not have a time limit, so I parked at a different entrance to my soaking hole canyon. There was already a car there, but I figured they had headed up, and not down. THe lack of footprints on the trail confirmed my thoughts. I strolled leisurely along a perimeter trail, and then began the hike up into the canyon. At one point it was super dark in the canyon due to trees and the sun being so low. It felt really cool and somewhat eerie.

When I reached the entrance to the springs canyon, I stopped and looked around for a while. I hoped to see the Owl, but I didn't. Way up on the ridge I noticed movement, and realized it was the other hiker and his dogs. I'd never seen anyone on the ridge above the canyon before while I was up there, so I decided to go slowly, knowing they'd probably be heading on down the trail that winds along the ridge. I came to the big pool and it was super dark and cold. The sun wasn't anywhere near being out at this location for another couple hours. I decided to hike up to the upper pool, which was ravaged in the massive flooding we had in the late summer. THere is a huge boulder that fell into the pool, and is right where it would be perfect to sit. The sun was shining brightly up in that part of the canyon. I kept lolly-gagging around, and finally decided to strip down and get in. The temperature this morning was about 37, and with the sun shining on me, it felt pretty balmy…except for the winds that kept blowing through. The wind was really whipping up on top of the mesas, but the canyon was more protected.

I got into the pool, and I wore my vibrams to keep my feet warmer. I soaked for about 15 minutes. My pup kept coming over to me and licking my face, which was sweet. Once I got out, I let the sun warm my body. I thought I'd hike straight out the canyon, and wondered if the old trail was still in place. My pup and and I scrambled way up into the grotto. It has been completely devastated by two wildfires and the flooding. The old spring vault is completely gone. Water just starts pouring out of the side of the slopes, which is really cool! Someone should take advantage of this water and bottle it.

I skirted around the overhanging cliff walls, and thought I'd just skirt along the edge of the cliff and climb out. The higher I climbed, the more I realized that I was getting myself in a precarious position. I did not have shoes with traction on, and the snow was like sugar…it did not clump together, and much of the scree, while frozen in place, was sketchy. As I looked around I decided I should not continue up, and so I snow slid down the steep slope and ended up backtracking along the trail. It was a good adventure. I felt real fear for a moment, which is good. I hadn't felt real fear in a while…just stupid stress-based shit.

I came home and fixed a shot of espresso. I beaded a necklace today, as well. The first time I've beaded anything in several years. My creative desire to make necklaces, dreamcatchers, and other pieces of nature-based art. At least I broke the ice.

### Shijin13 — 2014-01-02T17:53:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chrysanthemamas-journaling-journey.9218/post-104594>

yesterday - Havoc and I went for a walk midday. Chaos was down with the flu and DH stayed home with her (he was up w/her all night - such a loving dad)

Well on our walk we crossed the foot bridge that spans the little creek that runs through our neighborhood. at the opposite bank - there's a slate bench some one built - so we sat down upon it. about 5min into sitting a squirrel bounded up to us, stopped, sat up on his hind legs and began sniffing - and kept circling around... as if to ask what were we doing sitting in his spot. then he bounded away. 5 min after that he came back put his paws upon my shoe (I had moved my feet upon to the bench) - and stood there looking at me.

I think this little fellow has been fed by humans - its the only way to explain his behavior. I now think this might be the same squirrel that I have come upon during my walks - who shows no fear when I'm walking. I also wonder if he's the same squirrel that keeps coming to our house and bangs on the windows every now and again (he sits on the window sill - and taps his front paws on the windows - like he's knocking - silly squirrel)

Havoc loved it.

### nonchalant — 2014-01-02T18:31:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chrysanthemamas-journaling-journey.9218/post-104595>

Yeah, we ran into a chipmunk in Colorado that had been similarly trained. I have a picture of him sitting with his front paws in a begging position. He was adorable, I must say. And once in the Rio Grande park, we came across a pack of javelina between us and our campsite. We just stood there, and they just stared at us. Finally I picked up some rubble, and tossed it into the bushes in a neutral place. They all ran toward the sound, apparently thinking it was food. Sheesh. Are people going to start feeding rattlesnakes next?

### Chrysanthemama — 2014-01-03T05:15:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chrysanthemamas-journaling-journey.9218/post-104642>

Interesting! And funny, but in a not-so-funny-way. I'm sorry Chaos has the flu. If you are ever interested in super easy herbal remedies, let me know. Most of the time, herbs support the body to recover through illness, but they usually do not result in the immediate relief-from-symptoms that most people hope for. My experience has been that using herbal remedies during the flu and other illnesses helps to support and nourish the body, prevents illness from getting deeper into the system (thus, lasting for weeks), and generally makes people feel a little more comfortable during their illness. I'd happily provide you with some useful family herbal support!

### Chrysanthemama — 2014-01-29T00:23:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chrysanthemamas-journaling-journey.9218/post-107235>

January has been rough. I started out with great expectation and motivation, which was dashed pretty quick.
I have been doing regular CT sessions, though, and that is saving my soul and my brain. Probably even literally.
I need to start operating on quantum time, because this regular 24-hour a day thing is not working very well for me. I simply have too many things to do, and not enough time to get things done.
Lately, my biggest challenge has been fitting in regular workouts. I've always been super dedicated to my workouts, but lately it seems like if I want to CT, I have to forego my workout in order to do so. Even though I run to the spring and run back, sometimes incorporating a super steep hill climb if I take the longer route, but otherwise my schedule has been so packed to the brim, that my workouts are the first to slide. It sucks! I haven't lifted anything since October.

My homework for this week is to carve out a schedule that is going to work for me. When my stress levels start going up, as they have been because my job is still on the line, I find that I am not functioning as a Mom very well. So...I need to flow more, bend more, flex more. There is no way to balance all these things. That would be like juggling, which wouldn't work for very long. Bend, flow, flex, breathe.
