### sooperb — 2014-02-17T13:58:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-got-to-here.9395/post-110774>

With respect to the vibration, she isn't imagining it! Some years back I had gone to bed and was drifting off to sleep when I noticed a vibration. I was puzzled as I thought it must be the washing machine downstairs spinning, the kids often decided to do their laundry late at night. I couldn't fathom it. I had bought a book (one of my many hundred self help tomes lol) and I was reading that one day when someone described the very same thing, right down to the washing machine analogy, to say my jaw dropped is an understatement. I googled and eventually found a thread somewhere where this very symptom was being discussed, some describing like a cell phone buzzing in their pocket. No one seemed to know quite what it was, no medical doctor had been able to diagnose it. Interestingly, not everyone felt it in the same place, some had it in their groin, mine was at the top of my chest just under my heart area and went down to the stomach area. I thought it was my heart vibrating! Well, much as I hated the sensation there was nothing I could about it, every bedtime it would wake me at some point.

I think I finally found a connection that went some way to explaining the phenomena. The vagus nerve, which runs from your brain through your chest and down towards your nether regions. The cause of the vibration is almost certainly stress/anxiety. It's something that is triggered in the nerve that generates the sensation. It's not harmful but it can be frightening (I found it so for a while). As I conquered my anxiety, this symptom disappeared. It rears up now and again but it's not as strong, doesn't last more than a few minutes and I no longer worry about it. I take it as a sign that I need to address whatever may be causing my anxiety.

Your mother's GERD can also be both physically and mentally related. If she's obese, been taking antacids for a long time and suffers with anxiety, you have a classic collection of interdependent issues that feed each other. With respect to the GERD, someone linked to a website where the author has found multi-benefits from raising the head of the bed, the reduction of GERD being one of them.

This quote is from a Medscape article: A meta-analysis of previous non-randomized studies revealed that bed head elevation is an effective therapy in relieving the symptoms of reflux. \[9\] Elevation of the upper body at night is generally recommended for all patients with GERD. Here's the link: [http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/765033\_4](http://www.medscape.com/viewarticle/765033_4)

The website about raising the bed head is here: [http://www.inclinedbedtherapy.com/](http://www.inclinedbedtherapy.com/) The website is a bit untidy but the basics are explained along with a short video on how to raise the bed head. Personally I'd just be buying bed raisers lol. I don't know how you think your mum might respond to suggestions but there are several of us here over pension age lol and I am always game to give interesting ideas a try. Get her to sign up and read some of the threads and blogs, it may be just what she needs to give her hope and the gate to a path that leads to better health and all the benefits that brings.

### nicld — 2014-03-01T22:46:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-got-to-here.9395/post-112910>

Check out the brain-gut series. I know I have had gut issues for 10 years now. Bloating, significant constipation or diarrhea, weight gain, inflammation among others.

From Brain Gut 9 " **Non Geeks:** Our gut bacteria makes humans fat.

**Geek Alert:** The FIAF is made by our liver, muscles, and our small bowel wall when food sources are in short supply. This is the signal to stop storing fat in our fat cells when food is scarce. What is not well appreciated by many is that the FIAF in our intestinal wall is controlled 100% by our gut flora. When we have a simplified gut flora it favors fat cell creation. When our gut flora is [complex and healthy](http://www.sciencefriday.com/segment/06/15/2012/mapping-the-microbial-make-up-of-healthy-humans.html), it has 100 trillion cells with 250 species. We tend not to make fat in this instance either! Moreover, when the bacteria are active metabolically due to the presence of simple sugars, production of FIAF ceases, and fat creation is signaled. **This implies that our gut flora is directly tied to fat creation in humans**. The gut flora’s action is directly signaled to the brain via the afferent nerve fibers in the vagus nerve. In those newly created fat cells, a protein called **leptin** is also produced, and acts as a score keeper for the brain of how much fat is stored in the body."

When I started having gut issues, the weight lose that I was able to do on my own stopped and I have never regained it yet.
I am still waiting to get my GI Metametrix test back to see exactly what is going on.

Hi--I have lived in EMF HELL---didn't know it as that at the time. But, on March 29, 2013, my first night out of my high EMF bedroom, everything began to change for me. I call it "The Miracle".
For years, I had been doing everything I and my doctors (Naturepath/MD/Chinese Medicine doc.) could think of to be healthy, but was pretty much a "failure to thrive" and sliding down---weight loss ongoing, chronic fatigue, brain fog, gut cramping, bloating, diarrhea,constipation, and dampened immune system.
The only thing that seemed to work for me to help me feel better for awhile (always temporary) was acupuncture. That little nugget of hope was what started me thinking, researching everything I could find on energy, body, meridians, body electricity, etc.--led me to Becker et al., then, to getting my own Trifield Meter and getting a Bau Biology consultant to come out and do a follow up to what the Electric Company found--elevated electromagnetic fields in my bedroom (minimum 5mg at the center of my bed).

NOTHING I was doing really helped me UNTIL I BEGAN SLEEPING IN A CLEAN EMF ENVIRONMENT. I am recovering. Jack Kruse and his amazing work and his followers have helped me immeasurably. I am so grateful.

### Eliza — 2015-04-16T13:18:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-got-to-here.9395/post-161401>

Hi everyone

It's been some time since I've posted here, but I've been following the leptin reset (ketogenic, dairy free, gluten free) all of that time, except for a few indiscretions here and there....

I found a great doctor who has done a whole lot of testing and has been treating me with bioidentical progesterone and 7-keto-DHEA, along with about 1000 other supplements. However, I don't feel like I'm yet leptin sensitive, purely from the fact that I'm not losing weight (my bf is around 40% based on a BIA). I am coming close to running out of solutions with my dr, who thinks that I am leptin resistant, and need to have liposuction to get rid of the fat. I agree with her on a lot of things, but on this I don't.

I would be grateful for any advice anyone might have. I'm not sure quite where to go next, and think that I need to tackle my inflammation and gut dysbiosis really hard as a start.

I haven't been retested since I started seeing her, but here's a summary of some of the many labs I've had done:

I had a whole heap of antinuclear antibody testing done, all of which came up negative, except for SS-B, which came up positive, but is apparently of uncertain clinical relevance. So I don't seem to have autoimmune disease.

Hormones

I had some great hormone testing done which was really detailed, so I'll just provide the summary:
All measured in ng/mg:
B-pregnanediol - 873 (265 - 1612)
A- pregnanediol 171 (65-518)
DHEAs - 53 (23-252)
Androsterone - 563 (399-1364)
Testosterone - 7.4 (5.5 - 17.8)
5-alpha DHT - 1.7 (3.7-8.8)
5-alpha androstanendiol - 17.2 (22-66)
5-beta androstanendial - 23.1 (6-32)
Epi-testosterone - 8.1 (4.5 - 22.3)
Estrone (E1) - 5.1 (14-27.1)
Estradiol (E2) - 1.2 (2-4.9)
Estriol (E3) - 2.9 (5..6 - 23)
2-OH-E1 - 7.2 (4.6 - 14.4)
4-OH-E1 - 0.9 (0-1.8)
16-OH-E1 - 0.8 (1.3 - 4.6)
2-methoxy-E1 - 2.2 (2.9 - 5.9)
2-OH-E2
Prolactin - 105mIU/L (<540)
SHBG 103 nmol?L (20-155)
FAI <0.5 (0.5 - 5.0)

My 24 hr creatinine was within range

Cortisol
Appears to follow the right diurnal pattern (i.e. high in the morning, low at night), but it's above range overall
33ug (11-31)
Cortisone 95ug (49-131)
a-THF - 89ng/mg (134-281)
b-THF - 831 (783-1317)
b-THE - 1173 (1490 -can't read)
metabolised cortisol 2093ng/mg (2412-4509)
DHEAs - 53ng/mg (23 -250)
Melatonin - 30.276ng/mg (10-50)

I had anti-mullerian hormone tested separately, which came out high at 37.3. >30 indicates the possibility of PCOS, or increased risk of ovarian hyperstimulation in a stimulated cycle, or in post menopausal females - granulosa cell tumours). To clarify, I'm 29, and menstruating reasonably regularly, albeit lightly.

The clinical notes about the cortisol say that overall free cortisol levels are elevated, but metabolised cortisol is low. Cortisol clearance may be a bit sluggish. which keeps free cortisol levels elevated in spite of low overall production. The conversion between active cortisol and inactive cortisone favours cortisol, so a metabolic preference for active cortisol may be responsible for elevated levels of free cortisol more so than cortisol production.

On the upside - it seems my melatonin is good and the pattern is normal (even if the levels are either high/low). However, the levels of the sex steroids seemed to be mostly low across the board.

Gut testing - bioscreen faecal microbial analysis
Total aerobe count - normal - 1 x 10^11cfu/g (1 x 10^9 - 1 x 10^12)
Aerobe:anaerobe ratio - 1 (1-2)
Total aerobe count - 9.98 x 10^7 (1.00x10^7 - 1 x 10^8)
E.Coli - 87.05 % (70-90%)
Streptococcus - 9.14% (<5%)
Lactococcus lactis - 2.18%
Rothia mucilaginosa (1.63%)
Total yeast <1 x 10^1 (<1 x 10^4)

Total anaerobe count 9.99 x 10^10 (1 x 10^8 - 1 x 10^12)
Bacteroides - 67% (90-95%)
Eubacterium - 32.61% (<15%)
Lactobacillus - 0% (0.5 - 1.5%)
Bifidobacterium - 0 % (5-11%)

So overall there's an overgrowth of streptococcus, lactococcus, rothia and eubacterium and an undergrowth of bifiobacterium and lactobacillus.

Urine pyrrole analysis:
13 HPL ug/dL (10-15 is considered borderline, >15% considered elevated)
Sounds like my methylation pathways are really not working well?

Urine metals (24hr test)
Volume - high - 2.98 (0.5 - 2)
Creatinine - 11 (5.3 - 16)
Zinc - 14.6 (8-18)
Iodine - 59 (50-100 is considered a mild deficiency)
Iodine excretion - 175 ug/day (no reference range, I have no idea what's normal here)

HS-CRP 2.3 (<5.1). There's definitely inflammation going on! But hopefully with the ct, diet and other changes over the last year, this will have reduced somewhat on retesting.
Vitamin D - 70 nmol/L (51 -140)

Blood histamine 1.8 umol/L (0.2 - 2)
Copper 17umol/L (12-22)
B12 - 314pmol/L (>170)

Fasting glucose 4.5mmol/L (3.6 - 6)
Insulin - 3.9 (<14)
Cholesterol - 10.3mmol?L (3.5 - 5.5)
Triglycerides - 0.91 (0.5 - 2.2)
Urea: 6.4mmol/L (2.5 - 6.5)
Uric acid: 0.3mmol/L (0.15 - 0.40)
I'm pretty sure I'm badly dehydrated, but doctors here seem to look at me a bit blankly if I ask for a BUN creatine ratio.

Thyroid
FT4 - 13.6pmol/L (8.2 - 22)
TSH - 1.6 mU/L (0.5 - 4.4)
FT3 - 2.7 pmol/L (2-7)
I didn't have Reverse T3 tested at this time, but was previously highish - 370 (140 - 540)

Homocysteine 6.2umol/L (4-12.2)

Other things I noticed from a previous batch of testing: red cell folate - 2548 (>1300)

About my lifestyle: low stress, paying attention to circadian rhythms and light, trying to do around 45 mins in a bath with ice every day. Perhaps not enough seafood. In the last week I have started eating NZ green lipped mussels twice per day in an effort to correct.

I'm currently having a lot of supplements and meds to try to correct these problems:
\- 4% progesterone cream in 2nd half of cycle
\- 7-keto DHEAD - 25mg/day
\- metformin extended - 1000mg (500 am , 500pm)
\- ubiquinol - 100mg/day
\- ox bile - 500mg 2x per day (with biggest/fattiest meal)
\- NAC - 500mg/day
\- thyroplex (1/day). Contains 145mcg potassium iodide,50mcg selenium)
\- 300mg highly bioavailable curcumin/day
\-B5/B6 supplement containing 200mg B6, 1000mg B5 (calcium pantothenate)
\- 2 x 1000mg L-glutamine / day
\- 2 x 500 mg Vit C / day
\- Custom probiotics D-Lactate free

I am determined to lose this body fat, reduce the inflammation and move on using this new way of life. But my Dr has suggested perhaps I'm so leptin resistant that liposuction would get rid of fat, which would reduce the inflammation and allow me to lose the rest of the weight naturally. I really don't want to undergo lipo if there's another way.

A few things I'm confused about: my cortisol diurnal pattern seems to be normal (albeit a bit high in total, but low metabolised) and my melatonin is normal. Does this mean I'm not leptin resistant? I couldn't believe that with all the hormone problems and low T3 (and previously high RT3)

Hopefully my gut flora has seen some improvement on the above results since I started on the above probiotics. Is that where the inflammation is coming from? Do I just keep working on the gut with ct, or should I supplement iodine (through eating more seafood) and try some T3 therapy? Or is undermethylation my greater problem (from pyrrole testing, reasonably high histamine , not detoxing oestrogen properly etc)

Any thoughts on next steps would be so much appreciated!

Eliza.

Hi Eliza;

The clinical notes about the cortisol say that overall free cortisol levels are elevated, but metabolised cortisol is low. Cortisol

clearance may be a bit sluggish. which keeps free cortisol levels elevated in spite of low overall production.

FT4 - 13.6pmol/L (8.2 - 22)
TSH - 1.6 mU/L (0.5 - 4.4)
FT3 - 2.7 pmol/L (2-7)
SHBG 103
metabolised cortisol 2093ng/mg (2412-4509)
DHEAs - 53ng/mg (23 -250)
Cortisol Appears to follow the right diurnal pattern (i.e. high in the morning, low at night)
B-pregnanediol - 873 (265 - 1612)
A- pregnanediol 171 (65-518)
Estrone (E1) - 5.1 (14-27.1)
Estradiol (E2) - 1.2 (2-4.9)
Estriol (E3) - 2.9 (5..6 - 23)
Testosterone - 7.4 (5.5 - 17.8)
5-alpha DHT - 1.7 (3.7-8.8)
Iodine - 59 (50-100 is considered a mild deficiency)
Iodine excretion - 175 ug/day (no reference range, I have no idea what's normal here)
\----------------------
\======================
I'm currently having a lot of supplements and meds to try to correct these problems:
\- 4% progesterone cream in 2nd half of cycle
\- 7-keto DHEAD - 25mg/day
\- metformin extended - 1000mg (500 am , 500pm)
\- ubiquinol - 100mg/day
\- ox bile - 500mg 2x per day (with biggest/fattiest meal)
\- NAC - 500mg/day
\- thyroplex (1/day). Contains 145mcg potassium iodide,50mcg selenium)
\- 300mg highly bioavailable curcumin/day
\-B5/B6 supplement containing 200mg B6, 1000mg B5 (calcium pantothenate)
\- 2 x 1000mg L-glutamine / day
\- 2 x 500 mg Vit C / day
\- Custom probiotics D-Lactate free

\======================================================================
Number #1 if you want to loose weight you must have
under arm pit temperature at noon time 36.6C up to 37C
FreeT3 must be at about 75% within its range up to the top range
so current FT3 - 2.7 pmol/L (2-7) should change to:
2+0.75(7-2)=5.7 to 7

You are certified low on iodine/iodide
people associate iodine/iodine mostly with thyroid but
according to dr Fletchas
thyroid holds 50mg iodine
whole body holds 1500mg iodine
[http://www.allthingsmale.com/community/threads/rt3-high-how-to-deal-with-it-2.2666/page-23](http://www.allthingsmale.com/community/threads/rt3-high-how-to-deal-with-it-2.2666/page-23)

should supplement with selenium and Lugol's
while taking (- thyroplex (1/day). Contains 145mcg potassium iodide,50mcg selenium)
I assume that you at least have some selenium, so you can start with full dose of selenium and Lugol'sright away.
That would replace thyroplex

1/eod-->Super Selenium Complex, 200 mcg 100 capsules (lef.org)

1 dropperfull/day=48mg/day
Lugol's Iodine Family Pack -- 6 (2 fl. oz.) bottles
[http://www.amazon.com/Lugols-Iodine-Family-Pack-bottles/dp/B001RRP3FW](http://www.amazon.com/Lugols-Iodine-Family-Pack-bottles/dp/B001RRP3FW)

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Above dose is for 3-4 years.
iodide = halide=desirable
It takes that long to remove undesirable halides.
Watch out for halide exposure.
iodide=good
bromide
fluoride
chloride
xx

Right now you are low on FT4 & FT3
You could likely use natural thyroid hormone (T3 + T4 +T2)
I am thinking of 1-2 grains, but no more than 3 grains (at this time)
Say, enough to either get FT3 to desired (as above) level or TSH>=0.5 (or body temperature)
If you start building up to much T4 & FT4 without benefiting FT3 that would mean that deiodinase, enzyme converting T4-->T3 is not

working correctly. At that time it is good to know RT3. Raising RT3 indicates deficient converting process.
If body cannot make FT3 then it discard excess T4 via RT3.
Deiodinases work of selenium. But do not take more than listed above.
If conversion problems happens, the other way is to replace some natural supplemental thyroid hormone pills with T3.

Suggested Lugol's dose is =48mg/day, suggested by both dr's Fletchas and Brownstein
Except that dr Brownstein actually hesitates, thinking that 100mg/day bay be better in some cases.

I post it because there is controversy on this topic.
Also
minute (but vocal) minority is adversely affected (likely due to excessive halides, mostly bromide), so it pays to ramp up slowly.
Ramp up slowly, when having sufficient selenium=2-4 weeks

At this time you appear to have good free cortisol, values and shape.
That may change when you will actually begin to use it (the cortisol) more aggressively. Needs monitoring, plus
better steroid hormone panel management (below).

You are taking 7keto-DHEA. In this context 7-keto DHEA is helpful, but rather at 100-200mg/day dose

Monitor your (high) SHBG, it should get low with better FreeT3, if you would have big SHBG swings, your optimal FreeT3= minimal-SHGB

\===============================================================================
You have very nice hormone panel done (the panel is good but not the results).
But
to make proper adjustments I/you could use few other (blood) tests.
This blood tests you need to do periodically and rather often.
This blood tests must always be done at the same laboratory using the same type of test, so the results could be meaningfully compared.

Look at LONG LIST for LabCorp (items 1-19)
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/)

We are going to concentrate at first on day 2-4 of your menstrual cycle (minimum progesterone and estradiol)
latter
we will add days 19-21 (or max progesterone)

In the first phase you want to get this results on day 2-4 menstrual cycle:
pregnenolone=top of range or less
progesterone=1.5ng/mL or slightly more
cortisol(7:30AM) at top range, minimally more is ok
DHEAs=Women 275-400 μg/dL higher likely better (specially facing your low Testosterone, DHT and high SHBG)

To get there:
1---- use DHEA pills. I like DHEA 100mg from lef.org (because of good price)
You need enough pills to get the job done. (At the max I used 400mg/day, now 300mg/day, dr Kruse reported 500mg/day during some of his

hacks.
2---- keep progesterone that you are using in second half of cycle (make sure that you keep the dose unchanged)

3---- add
Nutricology Pregnenolone 150 Mg Sustained Release Tablets, 60 Count
[http://www.amazon.com/Nutricology-P...ref=sr\_1\_3?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1429018316&sr=1-](http://www.amazon.com/Nutricology-Pregnenolone-Sustained-Release-Tablets/dp/B000KII7CU/ref=sr_1_3?s=hpc&ie=UTF8&qid=1429018316&sr=1-)

Titrate dose watching pregnenolone, progestrone and cortisol(7:30AM) blood levels
Use as need. For perspective, you will need 150-900mg/day, I say, because I am a 75yo man. Man need much less.
Use above blood test to get to dose that will give you desired results.

After you get your day 2-4 optimal, or somewhat before, add monitoring of your days 19-21.
At day 19-21 you may want to check only progesterone and E2 (if finances are tight).

\-------------
When you will be close to optimal on 2-4 and 19-21 it it will be good time to have a look at your testosterone/DHT

In the mean time make sure that you do not take any medications athat affect aromatase and 5Ar enzymes.
Good dose of cruciferous vegetables is always a plus.

\--------------

When I look at you taking:
\-B5/B6 supplement containing 200mg B6, 1000mg B5 (calcium pantothenate)

I am thinking of replacing it with:

Methyl Guard plus from Thorne

Pantethine 60 Caps by Nutricology

[http://www.amazon.com/Nutricology-P...TF8&qid=1403534679&sr=8-1&keywords=pantethine](http://www.amazon.com/Nutricology-Pantethine-60-Caps/dp/B0001VKBOS/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1403534679&sr=8-1&keywords=pantethine)

+nutricology

\----------
\- ubiquinol - 100mg/day -----------> 400
\- NAC - 500mg/day -----------------> 2000

\-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

Please look at my posts here:
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/pg-e2-ratio.6427/)

get:
[http://spectracell.mybigcommerce.com/tests/](http://spectracell.mybigcommerce.com/tests/)
Micronutrient Test
Cardiometabolic Panel

It is really worth while to do this two tests, specially the micronutrient one is just one of the kind.
You would avoid 20 years of guessing by the biggest and best brains, it would help you tremendously.
Call their office, talk to them.

\-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Please post your whole body picture (scantily clothed).
Latter we will be able to check on your progress.

weight, height, ancestry

\-------------------------------------------------------

/////
You Mom's GERD.

She needs to have gastroscopy (and colonoscopy)
Sleep on very raised bed (or on the chair in sitting position)

GERD is often caused by low gastric acid (not high).

I have hiatal hernia.
Slept for the last 25-30 years on inclined bed.
Then lately started feeling discomfort in esophagus.
Got gastrocopy ----> slight irritation.
Got a deal from my gastroenterologist.
At night take Ranitidine 150mg (Zantac over the counter)
At day time up to 10 tablets of Betaine HCL with each meal (to get some acid)
Country Life - Betaine Hydrochloride with Pepsin 600 mg. - 250 Tablets
[http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B00068IY0O/ref=oh\_aui\_search\_detailpage?ie=UTF8&psc=1](http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B00068IY0O/ref=oh_aui_search_detailpage?ie=UTF8&psc=1)
That worked as a charm for the last year.
Last month I went back to flat bed and no Zantac. Works well, wish me luck.

////

.

### Eliza — 2015-04-17T12:03:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-got-to-here.9395/post-161501>

Hi Jansz

Thanks so much for all your advice. I really appreciate you taking the time to look through my labs and provide such thorough advice. You have such a wealth of knowledge - do you have a biochemistry or lab background if you don't mind me asking?

OK - so... tackle the hormones and thyroid harder. Do you think I should keep up with the same gut protocol? Will taking the D-Lactate Custom probiotics allow the better gut flora to proliferate once my hormone levels are better? (presumably I need progesterone in the top quartile before it has a better protective effect for my leaky gut).

As for my mum...I'm hoping that once I get better, I will be able to lead by example. It is amazing at how proton pump inhibitors, much like statins, are just given out by doctors like statins. As Jack would say, once you know better, you do better.

In terms of the labs, I'll do my best with getting them all done. A different doctor that I went to for totally separate reasons I asked for her to give me a lab sheet for some of the hormones (just a basic blood, not like the extensive testing above). I'll try to get those done next week which should give me a very broad indication of how much progress I've made in the year since I had the above tests.

Unfortunately, being in Australia, some of these diagnostic tests may be hard to get. I have a bad feeling Diagnostic Insight has gone, which did a lot of the Spectracell/Genova testing. I'll have to investigate more.

While I'm on the subject, can I also just say those in USA are very lucky that they can just buy DHEA. Here in Australia it's a controlled substance, and cannot be imported without a permit. I looked at the LEF one you recommended at about $25 - compared to the $115 I pay for the one I'm required to get prescribed and made at a compounding pharmacy!!

Many thanks again Jansz, I'll keep you all informed of my progress

### Eliza — 2015-12-10T10:23:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-got-to-here.9395/post-183832>

Hi everyone

It's been an interesting few months since I last posted. In August, I had an FMT (faecal microbial transplant) done, as my gut flora was quite simplified. I'd had a huge intake of antibiotics as a child, and the opportunity came up through a gastroenterologist in Australia, so I took it! I feel like that's really kicked off some improvements in my health, but I'm still not there yet. I have, however, lost 3-4kg body fat, which is a positive start (still around 43%, way too high though I'm not noticeably obese looking)

In November, I had some more labs done to see where my progress was at. I'm posting them here, so feel free to make any comments and as always, any advice is appreciated.

Cholesterol: 7.7 mmol/L (3.5 - 5.5)
Trigs: 0.82 mmol/L (0.50 - 2.00)
Urea: 4.5 mmol/L (2.5-6.5)
Creatinine: 65 umol/L (45-85) - I think that makes my BUN:creatinine around 15, which is an improvement from over 20 some time ago
Fasting glucose: 4.9 mmol/L (3.6 - 6.0)
Insulin (fasting): 4.4 mU/L (<14)
Iron: 15 umol/L (5-30)
Transferrin 2.1 g/L (2-3.6)
Saturation: 28% (10-45)
Ferritin: 100 ug/L (15-200)
HbA1c: 4.7% (4.3-6.4)
HbA1c mmol/mol: 28 mmol/mol (23-46)

Antithyroglobulin Ab: <10 IU/mL (<116)
Anti-thyroid peroxidase Ab: 7 IU/mL (5)

Vitamin B12: 282 pmol/L (>170)
Active B12: 102 pmol/L (>35)

Hormone Assays (day 2 of cycle)
FSH: 8 IU/L (2-23)
Progesterone: 2.3 nmol/L (<1)
Oestradiol: 202 pmol/L (80-920)
Testosterone: 2.6 nmol/L (<4.6)
SHBG: 127 nmol/L (20-155)
Calc free testosterone: 18 pmol/L (3-37)
FAI: 2 (0.5 -5)
DHEAS: 3.3 umol/L (1.7-11.5)
Free T4: 17.6 pmol/L (8.2 - 22)
TSH: 1.5 mU/L (0.5-4.4)
Free T3: 4.4 pmol/L (2-7)
Reverse T3: 521 pmol/L (140-540)
HSCRP: 2.3 mg/L (0-5)
Vitamin D: 90nmol/L (51-140) - hadn't been supplementing for months to give some context

Following these results, my Dr put me on 10mcg T3 to try to clear the high reverse T3. We also decided to retest my hormones on day 21.

I've just received the following results.
Day 21 hormone tests:
FSH: 4IU/L (11-30)
Progesterone: 114 nmol/L (3.7-45)
Oestradiol: 537pmol/L (80-1150)
Testosterone: <0.5 nmol/L (<4.6)
SHBG: 180 nmol/L (20-155)
Calc free testosterone: <2 pmol/L (3-37)
FAI <0.3 (0.5-5)
DHEAS: 2.1 umol/L (1.7 - 11.5)

Reverse T3: 290 pmol/L (140-540)
Free T4: 11pmol/L (8.2-22)
TSH: 0.67 mU/L (0.5-4.4)
Free T3: 7.2 pmol/L (2-7)

It seems positive that my reverse T3 has dropped so quickly on less than 2 weeks of a low dose of T3. My Pg:E2 also seems really high, around 200. Is this good, or too high? It was taken in blood. To give some context I have a 4% progesterone cream in the 2nd half of my cycle. My SHBG seems too high (a result of the T3? but was still highish in the reference range in previous tests) and I'm still not happy that my DHEAS are very low (I'm on 25mg/day 7-keto DHEA).

Any thoughts? I'm still eating epi-paleo most of the time, I haven't ct'd for a few months unfortunately. I have an office job surrounded by emf but limit my exposure at home and especially at night. I limit light after dark and pay attention to circadian rhythms. I have a normal cortisol pattern (though in the past it has been a bit highish in the morning).

7ketoDHEA should improve your thyroid work, but it will not increase your DHEAs.
Use enough DHEA pills to get your DHEAs real close to the top range.

Check your cortisol, either blood
or better in saliva four or more points during day

Check homocysteine level.

### Eliza — 2017-08-17T09:54:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-i-got-to-here.9395/post-221803>

Hi everyone

It's been some time since I posted on here, but as always, I've been reading all of Jack's blog posts with fascination. I've made a number of changes in the last few years, but I felt until I had an opportunity to change my environment, then I may not see huge improvements to my health, so I've kept away from pestering anyone for a while. Since I last posted on here:
\- I have had a faecal transplant via a gastroenterologist in Melbourne. This has helped my gut health massively!
\- As of 5 months ago, I have moved to a rural area where I can work from home, allowing me to spend at least 1-2 hrs in AM sunlight per day and away from all the Wi-Fi in a high-rise office building. I know I need to get out more now we're coming out of winter in Australia.
\- We've gotten rid of wifi in the house, though we may get one weak signal from the neighbours occasionally.
\- The gym where I go to has an ice bath set at 14c, allowing me to CT most days for about 25 mins (sadly in nasty water, but I felt the benefits outweighed the negatives).
\- I have a core magnetico coming on Monday!
\- I'm doing the leptin reset again for about the last 3-4 months (salmon sashimi BAB - admittedly farmed), with butter mostly as the winter fat.

So, with high hopes, I went and had some labs done a few weeks ago, and had any illusions I might have about my improved health shattered a bit.

I have some thoughts about what my problems are, but I'd be grateful for any advice on my labs, and corrections on where my thinking isn't right!!

hs-crp - 1.07 (ref: <4.91)
**BUN: Creatinine ratio (25:1) - terrible!!**
Sodium, potassium, chloride, bicarb, anion gap, urea, creatinine, eGFR, urate, bilirubin, AST, ALT, GGT, Alkaline phosphatase, protein, albumin, globulin, calcium, corrected calcium and phosphate all within reference ranges.
Serum iron: 15umol/L (10-30)
**Transferrin: 31 umol/L (32-48)**
Transferrin saturation - 25% (13-45)
**Serum ferritin - 163 ug/L (30-165)**
TSH: 2.7mIU/L (0.5-4.0)
FT4: 16 pmol/L (10-20)
FT3: 4.2 pmol/L (3.5-6) - unfortunately I didn't get RT3 tested - but I supplement 10mg T3 daily
Day 4 hormone tests:
FSH: 8 IU/L (2-12)
E2: 199 (100-530)
Progesterone: 1 (0.5-4.5) - despite 4% cream used on days 14-28 of my cycle
Total T: 0.7 nmol/L (0.5-2.2)
**SHBG: 94nmol/L (20-118)
DHEA-S: 1.9 umol/L (2.2-7.9) - yuck, despite daily supplementation of 20mg DHEA per day (didn't take it on morning tested).**
Calculated Free T: 5.8pmol/L (2.7-35)
25-OH Vit D: 75 nmol/L (51-200) - bit more pleased about this, as we're in winter here, and I've brought it up from in winter with just sunlight
**Total Cholesterol: 13 mmol/L (3.6-5.2)**
Triglycerides: 0.9 mmol/L (0.5-1.7)
**HDL: 2.1 mmol/L (1-2)
LDL: 10.2 mmol/L (1.5-3.4)
Non-HDL cholesterol: 10.6 mmol/L (.4)
Cholesterol/HDL-C ratio: 6 (<4.5)**
Hb, platelets and WBC all within reference ranges
HBA1C - NGSP 4.9% (4.0-6.0)
HBA1C - IFCC - 30 mmol/mol (20-42)
Vitamin B12:421 pmol/L (301-740)
Serum folate: 46.4 nmol/L (>9)
The thing is, prior to starting the leptin reset again, I had been eating mostly epi-paleo but moderate carbs, and my labs (especially hormones) still weren't impressive at all, but weren't as bad as what's above.
For example, in August 2016, I had the following results: (Day 21 of cycle)
FSH : 8 IU/L (2-23)
Progesterone: 0.9 nmol/L (3.7-45)
E2: 317 pmol/L (80-1150)
Testosterone: 1.5 nmol/L (<4.6)
SHBG: 130 nmol/L (20-155)
calc. free T3: 10 pmol/L (3-37)
FAI: 1.2 (0.5-6)
DHEA-S: 9.1 umol/L (1.7-11.5)
Cholesterol: 6.1 mmol/L (3.5-5.5)
HDL: 1.7 mmol/L (>0.8)
LDL: 4 mmol/L (.5)
Trigs: 0.83 mmol/L (0.5-2)
Tot/HDL 3.6 mmol/L (<5.1)
HS-CRP: 0.6 mg/L (0-5)
Free T4: 11 pmol/L (8.2-22)
TSH: 0.67 mU/L (0.5-4.4)
Free T3: 4.9 pmol/L (2-7)

My LDL cholesterol now seems to have gone crazy (which I get isn't necessarily a bad thing), but in this context, to me, it looks like my thyroid has stopped working (or is creating a lot of RT3). Is it just because it's winter? I have been CT'ing most days for around 20-30 mins. I have been trying to get as much AM sun as possible to help heal my hormones, but my DHEA has suddenly dropped in the last few months. Maybe due to inflammation? My ferritin levels and HS-CRP also point to inflammation.

But the cholesterol and hormone results always seem to be worse when I'm eating very low carb (a la leptin reset).

I have lost a little weight in the past few months doing the leptin reset (I weigh about 60kg, but still have a high body fat %), so perhaps some of that would increase the cholesterol?
Am I doing the wrong thing with CT and eating with the leptin reset? Or should I just be supplementing more DHEA, T3 and progesterone until the levels are better and then my leptin resistance will improve? I just don't seem to need quite the amount of supplementation when not eating as low carb.

I feel like in the first few weeks of the leptin reset + CT, I felt hot after most meals and was starting to feel really good. In the last few weeks, not so much, and my sleep has worsened a bit. I put it down to the time of the month, but given these last results I'm not surprised I'm not feeling great!

Any thoughts?? Thanks in advance!!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Gagnrad's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 360
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gagnrad — 2014-01-20T20:30:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-106306>

I'm currently about 20lbs overweight after drifting from strict Paleo over the last few months. Fortunately, it doesn't notice too badly, since I'm 6 foot, but I'd gone from 32" to 34" in trousers, and even those were beginning to get tight.

I've been purposely eating to be in ketosis since New Year. I haven't got a proper ketone meter. I'm generally up to light violet (8 mmol/L) or dark pink (4 mmol/L) on the ketostix, but how efficiently I'm burning β-hydroxybutyrate as yet I don't know.

Breakfast:

Cod liver oil 1tsp
2 oz beefsteak and
2 eggs fried in ghee
Â½ cup beef bone-broth
Â½ cup live sauerkraut
8 fl. oz. mug of coffee with 1tsp ghee and 1tsp coconut oil

I plugged that data into Fitday just to see. Surprisingly, it only came to 493Kcal (69% fat, 28% protein, 3% carbs). Usually, I'd eat rather more, but then I often eat an avocado, which comes to a fair bit by itself, or nuts or cheese with the meat and eggs.

Going to work was an unplanned adventure this morning. I got carded by the Royal Mail to say they'd impounded a package sent me as the sender forgot to put a stamp on it. Now the delivery office now doesn't open to the public till 7:30 but the train leaves at 7:39. Turns out the package was a magazine sent me by a charity I sent a cheque to. They should have been organised enough not to forget the stamp, but I guess I'll forgive them as it's a charity.

I end up sprinting up the footbridge to get over to the right platform to board the train, which is coming in as I get to the station. I may no longer be young, and I am currently carrying extra fat, but when I want to move explosively I still can. So I went up pretty much all out. I have done enough bodywork to be able to stay very "open" even when putting out extra effort and to stay focused and balanced and aware. All the same, it probably wasn't too bright an idea on a cold morning. But there didn't seem to be any frost on the steps, although it was a cold day. I lost traction just as I got off the stairs onto the level bit. Maybe it was the shift of posture, but certainly it was a little frosted up there. Fortunately I managed to land on the whole of both forearms (slightly more on the left), but I slid. The surface was like roofing felt with tiny grit in. I grazed the heel of my left hand and took a slight bruise towards the elbow. But that was all. I heard a girl hurrying up behind me call out, "Are you all right?" so I said, "Fine." She went past, then soon she's clattering down the other side to the platform. I was going slower down these steps, faster than normal, but staying light and balanced, because it's obviously far more dangerous to rush downstairs, and I could see they were a little frosted. Then I saw her go. Fortunately, she caught the handrail and carried on. The perils of trying to get to work on time …

As my hand had begun to drip blood, I tied a handkerchief round it. A fun way to begin the day. Painful, but only really superficial damage.

Salad dressed with olive oil and some wild red salmon for lunch, followed by 90% dark chocolate. I've got some fresh tuna for this evening.

I heard Nora Gedgaudas on a podcast the other day going on (at _excruciating_ length: she seems a very nice lady but I couldn't take the prolixity and gave up listening eventually) about mega-fauna, and even, so it seemed, implying that fish wasn't real Paleolithic food. But I love seafood, and it certainly seems to have been very important at some crucial stages in our evolution.

### sooperb — 2014-01-22T13:10:12+0200

I just baulked, cod liver oil, you are a toughie then  I bought a bottle years ago, took off the lid and OH and I tried to swallow a teaspoonful, we couldn't, it's awful. I applaud anyone that can.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-22T13:11:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-106609>

Got home yesterday evening to find that the heat-exchanger in my gas boiler had fractured, flooding the kitchen. We can all do with these kinds of issues on top of the everday ones, can't we?

Anyway, a heating and plumbing guy from a very local small but very reliable company came and drained the boiler and made safe for me.

I threw the contents of my fridge plus a few things on the shelves in a bag and am now staying with relatives for a few days till the kitchen dries out and the boiler gets replaced.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-22T13:14:40+0200

I get the fermented CLO from Green Pastures, as recommended by the WAPF. I generally go for the "Oslo Orange" which is flavoured with orange oil and not too bad.

It comes with a little syringe. I once made the mistake of squirting the syringe too hard: the oil hit the back of my throat, and, boy, does it burn if it does that!

### sooperb — 2014-01-22T14:07:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-106616>

It would have to taste of clotted cream before I could swallow it! I used take a capsule of codliver oil with butter oil but moved on to Krill oil as being purer with a higher EPA/DHA content.

Boiler trouble at this time of year is no fun, I sympathise. We once were away for a couple of weeks over a very cold Christmas, when we returned it was to find the boiler had burst some joints as had the shower fitting in the bathroom, water had been running for 2 weeks. Fortunately the boiler is in a shed outside and the bathroom was safe because the shower just ran into the bath. We had to have a new boiler, the old one couldn't be rescued but luckily the insurance paid, phew.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-23T10:23:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-106712>

Breakfast was an avocado and a large bowl of some beef stew followed by coffee.

I feel pretty lively, and the excess weight is still falling off.

I'm enjoying Dr. Kruse's book. Among other things the idea that diabetics are perhaps getting ready to go into a state of hibernation that never comes is fascinating. I remember this explanation being mooted in _Lights Out_. The new discovery about the marmots and polyunsaturated fatty acids that Jack mentions seems to make this explanation even more likely.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-23T11:53:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-106735>

Forgot to say. I've got a good anecdote about Australia back in the 1950s (I think) ... some while ago, anyway.

Back in those days, apparently, in places that served food you often got a meat pie with some kind of boiled veg that grew well there (marrows or something? I forget) used in place of potatoes. That was about it. So there was an American anthropologist out there studying the Aborigines and he stopped in a pub or cafe or something to order a drink and some food and asked for -- shock! horror! -- a salad. The barman said: "Salad? What are you? A poof or something?"

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-24T10:55:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-106889>

Breakfast: had the CLO. I fried a fresh tuna steak in olive oil and scrambled two eggs with a tbs of ghee, adding a dash of turmeric at the end. I also steamed about 10 small Brussels sprouts. Drizzled a little olive oil over the cooked tuna and the Brussels and squeezed some lemon juice over the fish. I then put a spoonful of fish eggs on top. Coffee (just plain black) after.

This was going some. I felt really full. I wasn't aiming for a Leptin-Reset meal, and would usually eat less protein that this, but this was a fair-sized bit of tuna (175g).

59 grams of protein in that meal, according to Mr. FitDay.

709 calories
59% fat
34% protein
7% carbs

Currently, my fat is usually 70 to 80% and my protein often less than 20%.

### Gagnrad — 2014-01-25T10:10:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-106998>

The weekend! Here's wishing a very happy weekend to everyone here.

Off for a hike today with friends and family. It looks a bit overcast, but I guess we'll be OK. I shall take waterproofs and a jumper in a pack but not over-dress. Cold is good!

Part of my fueling-up breakfast this morning was sausages. I didn't make 'em (easy enough in a burger-press, I know). I didn't even go to a butcher I'm afraid. These were "Sicilian-style" sausages from Sainsburys supermarket -- one of only two varieties of sausage they do that don't contain wheat.

I've read most of Jack's book now. Really interesting stuff. I've got "7 Viking Romances" translated from the Old Norse and 2 or 3 other books waiting on the side to read.

Breakfast:

tsp naturally fermented CLO
1/2 cup live sauerkraut
lambs' kidneys
tomato omelette
coffee
1 oz. almonds

That came to 607 calories, 70% fat, 22% protein, 8% carbs.

That must be closer to a normal balance of macronutrients (and nutrients in general) for a human than the usual breakfast. It does take time to prepare, though.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-04T12:45:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-108273>

Listened to an interview with Dr. Kruse on the optimal home on the Ben Greenfield podcast. Fascinating stuff. Come night-time Dr. K hits a kill-switch to disable all the electrics. He lights his place with old wine bottles turned into oil-lamps. Amazing. It sounds like a cross between a grotto and the batcave.

I'm now listening to an audiobook about the mountain men. One of the most incredible is Hugh Glass. He got mauled by a grizzly bear and was left for dead, but crawled out 200 miles to civilisation, stopping on the way to encourage maggots to eat decaying flesh on his back so that it didn't go gangrenous. How's that for survival?

### caroline — 2014-02-05T07:07:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-108408>

Wow ....that man had his wits about him!

### sooperb — 2014-02-05T19:17:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-108499>

The countryside in the bit of Lancashire I inhabit is flat and rather boring. Or there's the Bowland Valley, big hills and bleak. The east of the county is renowned for it's darkness and valleys.

Have you read Survival of the Fittest by Dr. Mike Stroud? He accompanied Ranulph Fiennes across the er, Antarctic I think. While the tale is gripping enough, as the Dr. he took regular blood samples. When they were analysed on returning home, it was discovered that at one point (where they were pretty much starving) they had no sugar in their blood so allegedly should have been dead. I think it ties in with a lot of what Dr. Kruse is saying. It's an old book but still in print or there's a Kindle edition.

While the tale is gripping enough, as the Dr. he took regular blood samples. When they were analysed on returning home, it was discovered that at one point (where they were pretty much starving) they had no sugar in their blood so allegedly should have been dead. I think it ties in with a lot of what Dr. Kruse is saying. It's an old book but still in print or there's a Kindle edition.

I think I may have. I definitely read a book that went through that journey; it may have been Fiennes' book rather than Stroud's, but I think it was Stroud's. I recall they took a lot of butter, sensible chaps, which they put in their porridge.

Got a couple of packs of the above Welsh, Soil Association certified butter yesterday. I'm going to make my own clarified butter this weekend.

### sooperb — 2014-02-05T19:47:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-108506>

We used to buy Rachel's, small family firm that was doing well. They got bought out  So now I buy Yeo Valley, their Greek style yoghurt is the best I've tasted, made with full cream milk and cream. We buy unsalted, raw French butter from Waitrose and occasionally Yeo Valley organic butter. That said, today I bought some Irish butter, in a flat shape a bit like Cheddar, but only because it was reduced from a silly £2.79 to half that. We like butter . I'm trying to work out how to do a sort of fast day just using CO and butter, the coffee will be part of it, but not sure how to eat the CO apart from coffee, I can manage small amounts off a spoon but I think a tablespoonful would be just a bit too much in one go.

Fiennes also took some of the larger sized Mars Bars for special occasions lol. It would be interesting to read Fiennes account, although he saved Mike's life on at least one occasion, Mike wasn't his biggest fan by the end of the trip. Despite that he agreed to run 7 marathons across 7 coninents with him at a later date.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-06T11:59:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-108680>

I bought some of the Heston beef stock from Waitrose -- it looks nicely gelled and seems to have nothing objectionable in it. I had a cup of that with my dinner with a tablespoon of coconut oil in it. Tasted OK, though it would have tasted better without!

Lambs liver with my breakfast this morning. It went OK with a couple of fried eggs, but it's really not fatty enough. I should have had bacon and liver and eggs.

### sooperb — 2014-02-06T16:03:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-108699>

Once upon a time I would have thought anything outside a traditional breakfast was the province of barmpots, now I am happy to try anything  Not sure about liver but OH eats kidneys sometimes so it's not such a big stretch.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-06T17:03:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-108705>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=108699)
>
> Once upon a time I would have thought anything outside a traditional breakfast was the province of barmpots, now I am happy to try anything  Not sure about liver but OH eats kidneys sometimes so it's not such a big stretch.

I think both kidneys and liver were quite traditional breakfast foods at one time.

I quite like both. I've tried marinating them in lemon juice, and I've tried marinating them in milk. In both cases it draws the blood a little and makes them taste less strong. I don't mind the taste of either just as they are, though.

What I haven't done is try liver raw. I bet Inger has! I'll have to ask her. It's said that once it's been frozen for 14 days it should be safe to eat raw! IIRC Bernard Shaw had to eat raw liquified liver when recovering from a vegetarian diet, having nearly killed himself with vitamin B12 deficiency.

### sooperb — 2014-02-06T20:42:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-108727>

Oh I wish that would happen to my sister! Daft tart was talking the other day about having made some casseroles for her grandchildren but had to wear gloves before she could touch the meat! Actually, now I think about it, she does have B12 deficiency along with pernicious anaemia, has to have injections once a month. I could put her straight but there's no way on God's earth she would believe, what do I know, I'm fat. One day, when I'm slim, I may just stroll past her and elevate a finger or two in her direction lol.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-08T12:33:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-109034>

I find quite often now I get a sense of well-being after eating. I suppose it must just be a case of my body getting what it needs at the right time. I had it again this morning. Breakfast was two large rashers of bacon (from the butcher, proper rind-on), a lamb's kidney, a fried egg, sauteed mushrooms with _herbes de Provence_, and Brussels sprouts, followed by black coffee. Maybe feeling good has something to do with the coffee, but I don't think it's only that.

I don't know how to describe the feeling. I'm sure it's not like that _"sleep after dinner, because you've had a sugar-crash after scarfing six potatoes"_ thing that many adult males get. Yet I want to say it's kind of "sleepy" -- maybe "sleepy without being sleepy". Not that "relaxed but alert" state -- just relaxed and "comfy" without being over-relaxed and feeling that everything is OK.

### sooperb — 2014-02-08T13:52:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-109037>

I think you mean "chilled" lol.

We buy most of our meat from a butcher in Ulverston in Cumbria when we can, he nearly always has Gloucester Old Spot bacon with rind and a thick halo of fat, Old Spot sausages and tonight's tea is pork chops. He practically has the names of the animals on his board and knows where all his meat comes from. It's a joy to me to be able to buy from him although I think we pay for his skiing holidays most years.

I'm just not sure about Brussels for breakfast, much as I like them . Are you steaming them, frying them or eating them raw?

That sounds like the place to go! Used to get similar sausages from a farmshop: I think the pigs were Old Spot/Wild Boar cross. Sausages tend to be a problem with ordinary sources, don't they, as they usually put "rusk" (i.e. wheat in them)?

> I'm just not sure about Brussels for breakfast, much as I like them . Are you steaming them, frying them or eating them raw?

I steam them but stop when they're still quite crunchy. I usually pour some olive oil or ghee over them -- just as another way to get in fat. They seem to go all right with stuff like bacon and eggs.

Bubble and squeak -- i.e. old potato and cabbage/Brussels fried up -- is a breakfast dish. So why not just Brussels? But, yeah, I should probably do something more interesting with them.

Oysters for breakfast this morning.

I shall try to get out and about at the weekend -- into the countryside, I mean.

I've been listening to a podacst about earthing this morning. This is something I'd missed hearing much about, so it's pretty new to me. Maybe I need one of those mats! I did try walking about without shoes a few years ago when that in some ways very misleading book _Born to Run_ by Christopher McDougall was out, but gave it up after I got a verucca and switched to just using Vivo Barefoot shoes, which have flat 3mm soles. That's all very well from the postural point of view, but if we have to take earthing into account ...

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-14T12:25:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-110149>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=110141)
>
> Oysters for breaky! that must have set you up for the day!

Yes, and yesterday a friend told me she was going to a great local fishmonger's and asked if I wanted anything, so I said oysters if they've got any. I hadn't been expecting to find any yesterday evening myself -- I did, that was breakfast -- now, since, her fishmonger had 'em too she brought in half-a-dozen today and I have some more to be going on with. An almost kruzian consumption!

> Have you thought of getting grounding shoes Mile?

I haven't looked into it yet. I did do a quick google search, and most of the ones I saw look very heavy and have raised heels -- like conventional shoes, in fact, except that they're grounded. But I don't want to discard the minimalist notion of what a shoe should look like, since I think that's sound for psosture and movement.

I did find some flip-flops -- thongs, as you say in Oz -- on Amazon that have a flat and fairly thin-looking and flexible sole that are grounding. They're not cheap considering they're just flip-flops, though:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Pluggz-Earthing-Grounders-conductive-black/dp/B00GJL6KH6/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/Pluggz-Earthing-Grounders-conductive-black/dp/B00GJL6KH6/)

Maybe. There's this notion that flip-flops are actually disruptive of gait, because you curl your toes to keep them on. I don't buy that. I think some people might, but I think if you think of leaving the shoe alone, and just let it slop around a little as you walk, they're OK. But 40 quid for a flip-flop is a bit steep.

I've had the 1000 miler Gumbies before, and they're less than half the price:

[http://www.gumbies.co.uk/gumbies-islander-flip-flops-black](http://www.gumbies.co.uk/gumbies-islander-flip-flops-black)

EDIT:

### sooperb — 2014-02-14T17:51:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-110209>

Oh yes indeedy  I have had a sheet about 2 years or more and it's on nearly every bed I lay myself to rest in. There's an earthing thread on the forum somewhere which, believe it or not, is how I came to the site, I signed up just so I could post on it. Then when I got some responses I started to take a look round the place and I've been here since. I described my experiences with it when I first started out and how I came close to abandoning it but was rather reluctant to because it was a big, single investment. I have an earthing mat too that goes under my bare feet when I'm at the computer.

The company I bought it from (BEP Technology) also do "spot" earthing tabs for pain and inflammation relief and I have some of those too. They are just like the tabs used to take an ECG and you can put them where you need to and then attach the earthing wire. I haven't done much of that but I have had a really painful shoulder so I tried it out a couple of days on the trot whilst I was sitting down for a couple of hours. At first it didn't seem to make any difference but this morning it's eased considerably, so either the earthing has helped or it's simply time, I make no claims lol but I had been taking ais and when I stopped, the pain came back.

The same company ([http://www.bioenergyproducts.co.uk/](http://www.bioenergyproducts.co.uk/)) do quite a few earthing products, they sell the pluggz, not any cheaper than Amazon unfortunately, earthing kits for cars and they have various anti-EMF gizmos.

### sooperb — 2014-02-14T18:35:23+0200

I did read it, Jack threw it at me "dirty electricity" lol. I bought an earthing rod to substitute for the domestic earth. ### Josh — 2014-02-18T18:50:44+0200

Note, he has publicly shared what many people feel is secret and not appropriate for the public, and he has been brash in the past with others in the community. From my limited experience, I feel he has done a good job and I am excited about the development of his network.

There are many great practitioners, less great teachers, starting at the beginning and starting right really helps...

### Josh — 2014-02-18T22:25:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-111068>

You will do yourself a big favor if you read Bruce Frantzis' "Opening the Energy Gates of Your Body" before deciding on a practice. It lays out the subject of who, what, where, when and why of Qi/Resonance cultivation in the human body. It is one of the most potent and actually dangerous things one can undertake. Consider that based on what we have learned, changing the vibration/resonance of the system fundamentally alters its function resulting in heaven and or hell depending on N=1, Conditions of Existence, whatever you want to call it....What are all of the EMF's doing??? Changing our vibration-rarely included in the promotional literature for Taoist and Yogic Arts, kind of like the disclaimers at the healthclub and parachute schools.....

### Josh — 2014-02-18T23:21:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-111085>

Well how are the people doing with the reset, CT, etc. who do not understand what the goals and purposes are and what gradient/timing is appropriate for them??? The point is not to move, but rather to move from a solidly rooted/grounded foudation maintaining correct alignment and energy flow. In traditional schools, all you do is stand for 1-2 years. Standing actually brings the greatest benefits and gains, but it is a mind killer until you can observe and participate in what is happening even without moving. This is hard to sell so most teachers skip it...It took me 12 years and 5 teachers until I got one who taught this properly with a thousand soft touches to adjust my posture and the occasional "that's it"....He could squat with hislegs at 90 degrees with no muscular tension. His teachers in Beijing made him watch in the park for 2.5 years before they would teach him. I believe the video is also him, right style anyway...

[http://taichidenver.com/whoarewe.html](http://taichidenver.com/whoarewe.html)

He learned the same system Bruce teaches. Bruce has been gracious to give a lot of perspective in his book so one can understand what is happening even if the teacher is poor at explaining it. Many people learn to embody and recognize the art without being able to explain it....

### Josh — 2014-02-18T23:40:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-111091>

Consider, the traditions that boast longevity promotion and health maintenance focus on the management of resonance first, not nutrition in the form of food. If one's resonance is in order, as Dr. Kruse has pointed out, food is not an issue. Not a small subject....Consider a lake with a weak wave pattern that any small breeze or stone falling into will change the pattern versus a lake with a gale blowing and 4 foot waves, nothing disturbs that movement of energy short of a bomb and even that, not for long....Yoga and Taoism have been working on these things for a few thousand years, we are kids in the sandbox with little more than 100 years of data and experience....Still to explain it and progress a synthesis is essential so here I am, the ever imperfect student.....For amusement consider that after I earned a red belt in tae kwan do at 19, I met a kung fu teacher in lower Manhattan who laughed and said that "they want to teach you to fight before you know how to stand, give me 3 years to teach you how to stand and then we can see about fighting"...That was 1988, I am still learning to stand up....He had started as a Shotokan champion, then the special forces in Vietnam and Cambodia. In Hong Kong, he stumbled on the Yin Family and managed to get taught in their temple for 4 years...Most families would not teach anyone outside of their family at that time or now for that matter. Understand when looking at Oriental family systems in medicine or martial arts, traditionally only the first son got the whole system......

### Josh — 2014-02-18T23:51:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-111094>

It comes to mind that many internal martial artists get big and this may be because the work alters their energy balance in a fundamental way. However, there are those that are tiny. Note the contrast between Bruce and his final teacher who could move him without leaving his bed or chair or touching him:

[http://www.energyarts.com/liu-hung-chieh-taoist-lineage-master](http://www.energyarts.com/liu-hung-chieh-taoist-lineage-master)

I met a man of this era of similar size in Hong Kong who simply detached my connection to the earth and threw me 20 feet across his Hong Kong apartment living room. As I am a student of Chinese history, I asked him how he had survived the "Hundred Flowers" and Cultural Revolution and he said "well once some gang approached him and his family and he gently put his hand on the leader and they left....Many masters were killed during these periods and there were street battles regularly, etc.. To have survived in quiet obscurity says something about the environment these people are able to create around themselves....

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-19T13:16:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-111166>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=111154)
>
> Hi Mike ..........Have you gotten your new books yet? I am anxious to know your thought on the cosmic Serpent..

It arrived yesterday, and I've now read it. I didn't really like it, because I was expecting to read a lot about the people and the world they moved in. I remember how much I enjoyed Dr. Kammler's book -- Survival at the Extremes (? something like that) -- which did just that. This book seemed mostly to be about the author's own theories, and some of the links seemed tenuous to me.

I was surprised he didn't mention the Old Norse -- maybe it occurred to me, because I'm fairly familiar with the mythology. There you also have the world-tree -- Yggdrasil -- and the world-serpent -- Jormangandr. I suppose these were left-over bits of older systems, the Old Norse being an agricultural people. Interesting the fear of "Lapp wizardry" in Iron Age Scandinavia. I guess there's something similar in Finnish mythology -- c.f. Kalevala where there's the struggle with the people of "North Farm".

I think there tends to be something of a connection between people's "form of life" and what their mythology and beliefs look like.

I don't think the writer establishes the connections he wants to make, and I don't think his book is anywhere near as interesting as Mircea Eliade's (which he leans on in places). Still, if Erwan likes it more power to his elbow: I certainly admire his skills. Maybe he sees things in I don't.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-20T16:40:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-111370>

This is fascinating. I knew that bone consisted of a protein matrix and minerals built onto that. I've just read in Robert O. Becker's book that what they found was that there are voids in both the calcium hydroxyapatite crystals and the collagen matrix just big enough to take a copper atom. Becker describes them as being pegged together by Cu atoms rather as antique furniture is held together by wooden pegs. Osteoporosis, in fact, seems to be a case of the pegs falling out. This might be caused by changes in electrical fields in the body or its environment. Wow!

### nonchalant — 2014-02-20T17:10:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-111374>

Yeah, he has some great drawings in that book.
I'm starting to wonder about hair. It's made of protein, right? It sure seems responsive to our state of health, for a "dead" substance.

From Wikipedia:

> The only "living" portion of the hair is found in the follicle. The hair that is visible is the hair shaft, which exhibits no biochemical activity and is considered "dead".

There is a cool pic of a cross section of a hair shaft on that page.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-20T19:36:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-111389>

I see what you mean. Becker says many fellow orthopaedic surgeons see bone as an inert framework and think of themselves as being like carpenters. This is quite mistaken. And too invasive an approach, too much pinning, too many plates can interfere with the self-healing properties of this (really) dynamic living system.

I'd not be surprised, if you were right to wonder about hair. There seems to be less "inertness" and more complexity, more mystery, and more potentiality in the body than people have reckoned on. It took a long time for people to accept brain plasticity.

And the kind of work Becker was doing could prevent publication, lose you a grant, or even cost you your job.

It seems to link in with a scepticism about reductionism. I'm thinking especially of what he says on p. 230. Interesting that he says there the Russians are sceptical of the more static, fragmented approach taken to biology in the West.

I find this persuasive. It reminds me a little of the view C. S. Lewis, J. R. R. Tolkien and Owen Barfield took of Baconian science. I'm sure that Saruman is partly a satire on this. He swaps his white coat for a coat of many colours -- because he can't see that a whole can be more than the sum of its parts. (C.f. white light/the rainbow.) Interesting that Tokien had a great love for trees, too -- just inert lumps of wood to many modern people.

I was also struck by something else when reading about osteoporosis possibly being the pegs falling out and this perhaps being down to changes in fields. It was this: doesn't Jack often say that people in Paleo think "it's all about the food" but it's not? When you start to read the books he's recommended, you begin to see where he's coming from.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-21T10:03:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-111494>

I've nearly finished _The Body Electric_ now -- one chapter left. It's a gripping book. I should imagine it would be impossible for anyone to read it and feel blase about EMF ever again. Not that he spends a lot of time talking about that but simply that he shows how fundamental to life electricity and magnetism are.

Lots of interesting snippets. E. g. the senior doctor who's an Army colonel who comes to him and says: we know acupuncture works, but we don't know why; do you think it may be on account of this? It turns out it probably is.

It's equally remarkable -- but not in a good way -- how much resistance Becker met, not because there was anything wrong with his techniques or experiment-devising but simply because what he was finding didn't fit in with people's preconception of how things were.

A friend, a physicist, remarked to him that "biologists are the worst".

Some of these people seem to have been utterly mired in _ideology_ -- there's no other word for it -- and so immune to evidence. Questioning their orthodoxy was like questioning communism in Albania or North Korea.

It's interesting that practical people, like soldiers and policemen, can be very much more open-minded than scientists. So, for example, some police forces will ask psychics for help to find missing persons. Now, doubtless some psychics are bogus -- Harry Houdini spent years looking for a genuine medium and never found one -- yet sometimes they do seem to be able to help. Does it really matter if we understand _how_ they do what they do? Being determined not to believe anything in advance, because you've decided what they say they can do doesn't fit in with your picture of the world seems asinine to me.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-21T19:28:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-111572>

My 25-Hydroxyvitamin D bloodspot test results came back.

103.6 mMol/L. (Or 41.5 ng/dL).

This is twice the level described as "adequate" by the NHS but around what many doctors advise. Many people in the ancestral health movement reckon on something more like 150 mMol/L, though. So a good reading, but it may not hurt to be a little higher.

This reading is against a background of taking most days 1/2 teaspoon to a teaspoon of cod liver oil (usually the fermented one) and sometimes a 2000 IU capsule or two of D3.

I'll probably go up to two capsules (4000 IU) regularly till the winter's over. A common suggestion seems to be 4000 IU in winter, 2000 IU in spring and autumn, and none in summer (get out then).

### caroline — 2014-02-22T02:01:41+0200

sorry you didn't like the book .... I will have to buy you a coffee one day! feel free to ignore any further book recs.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-22T08:50:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-111670>

> [Julie5 said:](/goto/post?id=111633)
>
> So is the Body Electric terribly difficult to read? Doc K recommended it to me when I asked about my situation. EMF seems to create a great divide between hubby and me. I want to make some changes in our home; he doesn't want to. We do turn off the bed/bath breakers at night and I just ordered a red light bulb for reading at night. Baby steps with hubby. This morning we sat together on a cotton blanket in the yard, bare foot in the freezing cold, an watched a beautiful sunrise happen

I think it's mostly easy to read but not all. He does get round to the dangers, but he saves that up for chapter 15 -- the last main chapter.

The tale of the USSR bombarding the US Embassy in Moscow with EMF rays I'd heard something about before. Lots of illness, concentration problems, eye-problems. Three ambassadors seem to have contracted cancer. What Becker says is that they set this up after a joint conference where all the Russian speakers had addressed the dangers of EMF seriously, and all the US experts had virtually said there were none -- as seems to have been believed in the US (and which suited industry and government).

What the Russians did was to range up and down different frequencies and so on but always keep the strength of the signal below what the US had itself set as the safe limit (on the advice of a guy called Schwann the US got off the Germans after WWII). It was like they were deliberately mocking them for not understanding that EMF is serious stuff.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-23T10:36:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-111776>

Nothing much going on.

Another 2lbs lost this week. Seems to be the average amount now.

Couldn't eat dinner last night. I felt a little sick. Maybe it was the oysters I had for lunch. There again maybe it was drinking a glass of cider in the afternoon when I'm not so used to it. As likely, neither.

I didn't drink all January. I've been trying to stick to one small glass of wine every other day and only with meals. More is tempting, though. Trouble is, besides not being very healthy it'll probably stall weightloss. Besides, it tends to send me to sleep.

A warm then a cold shower. Breakfast was an avocado, then bacon, eggs and a grilled tomato. I thought I wouldn't mind a little more so I had a few hazelnuts and some dark chocolate. Coffee.

Now I'm reading some anthropology. This was new to me, or if I knew I'd forgotten:

> Aborigine to anthropologist: "My word! Dat _putjikata_ proper good tucker!"

Here _putjikata_ is a word in an Aboriginal language derived from "pussy cat".

Short walk on the hills yesterday. Muddy shoes now.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-23T11:55:03+0200

Another interesting snippet.

Anthropologist visits the Carnavon tracking station in Western Australia. (Late 1960s?)

> It did not help my confidence in the local personnel to look out of the windows ... and see the thirty-foot dish of the Unified S-Band radarscope swing wildly across its azimuth as its operators, for a bit of fun, shot crows out of the sky with the powerful radio pulses.

Sounds like a safe piece of equipment ...

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-23T12:08:03+0200

I've nothing better to do. LOL

I was intrigued to read about what was apparently Australia's most popular crime writer when this anthropologist was over there. A bloke from the Outback called Arthur Upfield. Apparently, he has a part-aboriginal detective called Napoleon Bonaparte. There's stuff in these books like remote towns with five-foot high reefs of beer bottles around them, and fascinating accounts of the knock-on effects of drought on animal populations, and it's all based on truth.

I must try to find his books. I like crime now and then. I was a big fan of Montalbano -- mostly because of the humour in it and the picture of Sicily in it. I've gone off Andrea Camilleri now, though. Each book seems to get darker and darker, nastier and nastier. I like the sunlight and the sea and the wit.

I read all the Arthur Upfield books many years ago. I'm afraid those novels and Bruce Chatwin's _Songlines_ formed my very limited view of Australia until I studied Anthropology in college...I went through hundreds of detective/crime books in my teens and twenties....started to get out of it when most of the new authors went very bleak and dark....the sparkling, humorous who-done-it seems to have disappeared..The closest to this style that I've found recently is _The Sweetness at the Bottom of the Pie_...written by an American set in 1950s England with a young female protagonist who is a chemistry genius. They are very light and silly but it makes a nice change from dark and nasty.

Have you ever read Robert Barnard? They were often very funny too.

### Gagnrad — 2014-02-23T16:32:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-111806>

> [SeaHorse said:](/goto/post?id=111790)
>
> I read all the Arthur Upfield books many years ago. I'm afraid those novels and Bruce Chatwin's _Songlines_ formed my very limited view of Australia until I studied Anthropology in college…

Chatwin projects a rather rosy view of hunter-gatherer societies, doesn't he? (He had a bee in his bonnet about pastoralists, though.)

They're certainly admirable in many ways -- _that's_ not the issue -- but they don't spend all their time sitting around admiring lotuses and kissing babies. _Some_ tribes seem to have been relatively peaceful; others, anything but. Plenty of fights over women, occasional violent irruptions into neighbours' areas, expeditions to prove one's courage by seizing property or taking life -- interestingly, the Apache had two terms for war, meaning something like raid-to-seize-property and raid-to-kill-enemies -- and so on, are common enough in the record. This comes of coming on the scene after most of them have, sadly, gone or been pacified by more populous and technologically advanced societies. If Chatwin had gone around wandering where he was not wanted a century ago, he'd have quite likely ended up with a spear in him -- the same, of course, for any Aborigine from a tribe different to one whose territory he'd strayed into, unless, perhaps, he could prove some kind of kinship.

It surprises me that some paleo enthusiasts in the U.S. tell this comfortable city-dwellers myth to each other. (The ridiculous Jared Diamond has told it, too. Diamond even has hunter-gatherers as proto-feminists!) Americans ought to know more about the life of the people they displaced. It's the history of where they live; and it's fascinating in itself. Nothing, for example, for a party of Sioux warriors to fall upon a straggling line of Pawnee women and children, on the move while their menfolk looked for game, and wreak wholesale slaughter, as George Bird Grinnell relates. Killing a woman wouldn't get you as much _kudos_ as killing a man among Plains Indians, because it's easier, but still worth a few points …

But false romanticism or not the _Songlines_ is still a good read. I also liked Laurens van der Post's _The Lost World of the Kalahari_, and I think it's now pretty clear that anything Sir Laurens' said about anything needs to be taken with a grain of salt. It doesn't stop the book being enjoyable.

> I went through hundreds of detective/crime books in my teens and twenties....started to get out of it when most of the new authors went very bleak and dark....the sparkling, humorous who-done-it seems to have disappeared..The closest to this style that I've found recently is _The Sweetness at the Bottom of the Pie_...written by an American set in 1950s England with a young female protagonist who is a chemistry genius. They are very light and silly but it makes a nice change from dark and nasty.

I'm glad you like the witty crime novels, too.

> Have you ever read Robert Barnard? They were often very funny too.

No, I've not heard of him.

### SeaHorse — 2014-02-23T19:07:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-111831>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=111806)
>
> Chatwin projects a rather rosy view of hunter-gatherer societies, doesn't he? (He had a bee in his bonnet about pastoralists, though.)
>
> It surprises me that some paleo enthusiasts in the U.S. tell this comfortable city-dwellers myth to each other. (The ridiculous Jared Diamond has told it, too. Diamond even has hunter-gatherers as proto-feminists!) Americans ought to know more about the life of the people they displaced. It's the history of where they live; and it's fascinating in itself. Nothing, for example, for a party of Sioux warriors to fall upon a straggling line of Pawnee women and children, on the move while their menfolk looked for game, and wreak wholesale slaughter, as George Bird Grinnell relates. Killing a woman wouldn't get you as much _kudos_ as killing a man among Plains Indians, because it's easier, but still worth a few points …
>
> .

That is certainly true that most Americans are pretty fuzzy about the indigenous world they disrupted. But, somebody like Grinnell was commenting on the behaviours he saw many years after the contact with Europeans started. Think of how the horse changed the plains culture....it stratified the tribes, created more movement, more war, etc. And of course even lumping all the Native American cultures together is kind of ridiculous as there was as much variation among them as there was in any other huge geographical area of the world.
You might like Wade Davis's book _The Wayfinders: Why Ancient Wisdom Matters in the Modern World._

People are how they are and why pretend otherwise? I think I do know why ... People do it, because they think others will admire them for it. Pretty silly reason. The truth is more important. Can't stand know-nothing prima-donnas, like ... Well let's not name them.
