It indeed would be. Who does that?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-02T16:12:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-113061>

I'm a little annoyed. The darn EU mafia has inconvenienced me, and cost me, again. I have to pay £12 in "customs fees" for a parcel. I'll return to this in a minute.

Meanwhile, I'd add that I've nothing but contempt for the EU, a shameless exercise in empire-building and graft for politicos. It's interesting how all the failed politicians -- and sometimes their whole families -- end up with sinecure positions in this appalling organisation. Edward Heath lied to the public, and years later actually admitted to doing so with a nauseating giggle, in order to sell this country's sovereignty out to this odious organisation, claiming it was just an economic alliance while knowing it was intended to be much more. They try to frighten the public by asserting that life is cold outside the gang; meanwhile, countries like Norway and Switzerland, that are sensible enough to do so, do very well. Did I hint at corruption with that word "graft"? Yes, this is a familiar part of the story:

> The outline of her story has long been familiar. In 2002, as the first qualified accountant to be given the job, she was appointed to sort out the EU's accounts, which for six years running had not been cleared by the EU's Court of Auditors due to a maze of "irregularities". ...

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/comment/...01009/A-look-in-the-EUs-unbalanced-books.html](http://www.telegraph.co.uk/comment/columnists/christopherbooker/5301009/A-look-in-the-EUs-unbalanced-books.html)

Anyway, I ordered some little blue-light filter screen covers from Sleepshield, who are based in the U.S..:

[http://www.sleepshield.com](http://www.sleepshield.com)

Since these come from "outside the EU", they have been impounded by the Post Office and I have to go and pay "customs duty" before I can take possession of them. Sickening. How's that for restraint of trade? It's very unfortunate since no-one in the U.K. seems to be making anything similar and no-one is importing these in bulk. Hence, I'm caned on the orders of that mafia organisation commonly known as the EU.

### sooperb — 2014-03-02T17:19:37+0200

You have my sympathy on that one Mike, not only will you pay customs duty but you may also be liable for the Post Office's handling fee! As for the EU, I'll join you on the nothing but contempt for bench. I loathe them with a passion, their interfering, the odious money grubbing self-promoting officials whose only mission seems to be to see just how much gullible members money they can systematically milk to line their own pocket. I really don't understand how anyone can possibly think that we should stay in. Constraint of trade doesn't cut it with me when our own country is importing from the far East and putting our own businesses out in the cold. ### Gagnrad — 2014-03-03T11:27:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-113151>

Up nice and early as usual after a long, dark, and cool sleep, following an evening that ended with natural light from candles and meditation. I must be Epi-paleo or something.

Breakfast was an avocado split in halves and with French dressing, followed by an omelette with some peppers and onion in, some Italian air-dried ham and coffee.

I'm wondering about oil lamps. Here are some "High Quality Oil Floating Wicks made in France since 1838" from beeswax:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/FLOATING-WICKS-MADE-FRANCE-SINCE/dp/B00AXKE896/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/FLOATING-WICKS-MADE-FRANCE-SINCE/dp/B00AXKE896/)

All you need seems to be some kind of glass container -- the usual thing would be "votive glasses"

[http://www.pilgrimgifts.co.uk/images/stock/F-VG014R.jpg](http://www.pilgrimgifts.co.uk/images/stock/F-VG014R.jpg)

... available at Amazon and all kinds of places. You can put "night lights" or "tea lights" as they're sometimes called in these (though they're often designed for larger "24-hour candles"). But you can also put a light olive oil in and add one of these floating wicks. The Russian Orthodox burn votive lights in front of icons and Roman Catholics and Anglo-Catholics use them in front of statues of saints.

Here's a old Roman oil lamp -- this one, according to the page I took it from, apparently depicts Hercules ... or possibly Ulysses with Circe. (I can't see any seamen turned into pigs, though.):

### sooperb — 2014-03-04T11:55:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-113316>

Am I alone in thinking, what a waste of butter lol? Yak or otherwise. I know, you utilise whatever you can to meet a need but I was brought up with the notion that butter was expensive and for special occasions only. When I think of what I've used instead !

Have you been to Tibet or Nepal Audrey? A neighbour went trekking there and she reported quite spartan conditions. Isabel Losada's book, "For Tibet, with Love" is both a travelogue of sorts and a report of the situation there, I find it hugely sad what the Tibetans have to go through

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-04T12:19:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-113321>

After the invasion of Tibet, the Chinese apparently declared that wheat, which is widely grown in Northern China, was the proper Chinese crop, and the Tibetans were not to grow barley any more. The crop failed: it's too cold for wheat there, and there was mass starvation. One brutality among many. Tibet's an appalling tragedy: I can't see there's any possibility of the Tibetans ever regaining their freedom, a country simply wiped off the map.

And, of course, everyone looks the other way -- not that they could do anything anyway. Our Prime Minister has, apparently, been told by the Chinese that he is not to meet the Dalai Lama again, having done so once. He seems to be acquiescent. Can you imagine anyone telling HMG what to do in Palmerstone's or Gladstone's day?

### sooperb — 2014-03-04T12:28:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-113324>

I am appalled at what's happened in Tibet, pretty much genocide by stealth and the west does nothing. There we go rushing into Iraq, Afghanistan and probably we'll have a presence in Ukraine soon no matter how much they deny it, yet Tibet is left to rot. I'm not very politically minded but I can't see the justification in ignoring their plight. I suppose no one wants to challenge the Chinese. I think it's too late anyway, they hold so much economic domination, not least in the UK and they are moving into Africa now and they are already in parts of Malaysia. I suspect we have lost the war before it's started.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-04T13:06:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-113327>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=113324)
>
> I suppose no one wants to challenge the Chinese.

They only thing they respect is force, and no-one wants a Third World War. It came close enough in Korea: McArthur actually wanted to use nuclear weapons there. Truman relieved him of his responsibilities. This is why the U.S. never invaded North Vietnam: the Chinese wouldn't have stood it. And the U.S. could never effectively interdict the flow of Chinese weapons coming down the Ho Chi Minh trail. So the powers danced around each other and went as far as they dared.

The U.S. did train and supply Tibetan guerillas, which was indirect and deniable. But part of the price for the Chinese shutting the Vietnam war down -- telling the North Vietnamese to come to the table and sign accords -- was that the U.S. stop doing that.

Nothing anyone can do. It would be nice if people would at least _speak_ to the Dalai Lama though.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-05T15:13:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-113577>

This morning I noticed that a small red patch on my chest had gone.

Just a minor skin problem. Something that's been there in the recent past now and then -- I think at times when my diet has not been quite "clean", as they say in Paleo, or perhaps when I've tended to drink a little more than's ideal.

Not even a slight difference in skin-tone -- I couldn't see any indication that it had even been there.

The interesting thing is that I've been back stringently observing a low-carb paleo diet since New Year's Day. So, assuming that did resolve it, it's taken _nine_ weeks to do so. I think some changes can be quite rapid, but some can take longer to happen -- except perhaps for children.

I'm still waiting for the eczema to depart from my ears.

### caroline — 2014-03-06T00:35:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-113662>

I would layer this ointment on and then put sox on my hands and go to bed - my hands would be perfect in the morning! ... not very sexy tho...

wait for it ..... it is an ointment for ... diaper rash!

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-06T09:05:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-113729>

New Zealand lamb last night. Big mistake.

I wanted a change, and I hadn't had lamb for awhile. I like the local lamb and the Welsh lamb is very good. I had a large chop couple of weeks ago and it was as good as a steak, or better, very flavoursome and juicy.

But the NZ lamb tasted like damp cottonwool. Maybe it's because it's been frozen a long time. To add to my dissatisfaction they'd cut virtually all the fat off, too. I've mentioned this as a new problem I've noticed with meat bought in supermarkets. Some of the packets were actually labelled "New Zealand _supertrimmed_ lamb". I know what "supertrimmed" means: it means they've cut all the healthy fat off. I fried the meat, put a lot of ghee and olive oil on my kale and ate an avocado for good measure, so I probably made up for it, but I shouldn't have to do all that. I should just have been able to eat the fat that _should_ come with the meat.

I shan't bother with lamb again unless it's good quality local/Welsh lamb from a butcher and comes with its own fat.

### caroline — 2014-03-06T10:12:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-113734>

Your Welsh lamb sounds awesome - is it hard to get? expensive? are you a good cook?

My trip to Tibet..... We started at the top part of China \[Kashgar\] and then took 4-wheel drives towards the plateaus and holy mountains of Tibet - ascend the Xinjiang-Tibet Highway - the highest road in the world.

This isn't normally the way tourist enter Tibet so there is no tourist infra structure. We commonly stayed in Truck stops. We went to base camp at K2 and then made our way eventually to Llasa - to see the Potala Palace etc.

The borders can close at the drop of a hat - we were already in Tibet when an American woman did something stupid at K2 ... and the borders were closed. 4 People died on Everest that weekend we were there.

There were 3 couples from the UK on our trip.........

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-06T10:31:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-113737>

The lamb from the mountains is famously "sweet" perhaps because of the sheep breeds used there or the herbs in the grassland.

The "The War-Song of Dinas Vawr":

> THE MOUNTAIN sheep are sweeter,
> But the valley sheep are fatter;
> We therefore deem’d it meeter
> To carry off the latter.
> We made an expedition;
> We met an host and quell’d it;
> We forced a strong position
> And kill’d the men who held it. ...

[http://www.bartleby.com/246/103.html](http://www.bartleby.com/246/103.html)

Of course in Thomas Love Peacock's day, fatter animals were obviously more desirable. Before all this nonsense ...

I tried googling "Welsh lamb" and found this -- makes your mouth water:

> Our Extra Special Welsh Lamb comes from the lush, natural pastures of North Wales mainly from the Conwy Valley, Anglesey and the Vale of Clwyd.
>
> These lush pastures provide ideal conditions to produce what is arguably some of the finest lamb available anywhere. With full traceability you can be sure of the highest standards possible of animal welfare and quality. ...
>
> Salt Marsh Welsh Lamb from the salt marshes of Anglesey, our lamb was recommended by Rick Stein in his Food Heroes TV series. ...
>
> During the later part of the year Welsh Mountain lamb becomes available. This hardy and indigenous breed is full of flavour, grazing pastures that have never been farmed in the modern sense. ...
>
> Our lamb has always the correct amount of fat cover for succulence and taste. The meat is then matured for a minimum period to ensure tenderness. ...

[http://www.edwardsofconwy.co.uk/shop/welsh-lamb](http://www.edwardsofconwy.co.uk/shop/welsh-lamb)

That's _real_ meat.

I'm not really a cook myself, but I like to take some care.

Your holiday sounds superb. Did you take lots of good photos?

### sooperb — 2014-03-06T11:28:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-113742>

Welsh lamb, mmmm. Marsh bred lamb, mmm. Lamb in general, mmmm. Salt marsh lamb from Mont St. Michel, a local delicacy although the French are fond of hyperbole for their produce lol.

Our butcher sells Herdwick lamb from the fells, we tried it, a bit tough and not such a good flavour. Slow roast shoulder, try Ottolenghi's recipe, wonderful. I'll swap you a bottle for the champagne they make just down the road here. They're not allowed to call it that, of course, but it's beaten French champagne in blind tastings. The winemaker told us that he doesn't even bother to try making red wines, though -- just not hot enough here.

> I love your signature .... have you changed it? or is this the first time I stopped to read it.

The rest is always raining here.

Don't embarrass me! I've read a bit not a lot ... and more than I would have done if I had better things to do. I haven't actually read Traherne, although I used a quotation from him in my signature. I started it, but lost interest. But he does have some poetic passages.

### sooperb — 2014-03-07T14:16:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-113984>

He makes me feel a long way behind too. My problem is too much TV and computer time. Plus a husband who insists on speaking to me when I am reading and who then complains when I have no idea what he said because I was submerged in a book. He reads things out from the computer, says, "Look at this" or "have you seen this "? He has no idea how annoying I find it but I know if I tell him I will get a wall of silence instead.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-07T15:23:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-113988>

I've been trying "Angriest Trainer" podcast. He is pretty funny. He was Evan Brand's latest guest, so I thought I'd try his podcast. Found it easily in iTunes.

This is a guy called Vinnie Tortorich, who is a Hollywood trainer, and who has a runaway best-seller about his life and about health and fitness. Apparently, he couldn't find a publisher to bring the autobiography out, but self-published it's got out there and it's struck a chord with people.

It made me think of Lance Armstrong, since Vinnie has also done a lot of cycling and has struggled with cancer. He'd need a bit of beeping if they had him on telly, but he's not in the Ozzy Osbourne class. By the way, how is Ozzy still alive?

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-08T13:38:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-114133>

I bought some Union Coffee this morning. This one was a special single estate Brazilian coffe they do. It's supposed to be "better than organic". They do all kinds of things like not pruning the bushes too much and allowing trees to grow amongst them rather than forcibly monocropping across the whole plantation.

I tried a cup brewed as espresso in the coffee shop, and it was pretty good.

### caroline — 2014-03-09T00:43:27+0200

BTW - good morning!

I forgot to tell you .... the other day when I went to see the empath .......she said one thing I didn't understand.

She said I would maybe be going to England and she talked about the castles etc. or at the very least it had some significance. She spent some time talking about and describing this and it seemed to be important.....

I had just been posting to you before I went to meet her .......coincidence????

I still can't figure it out...... I love England .... but I have zero money - so I am not going anyway!

Astral projection? I have some very vivid if weird memories of this. For years I was convinced not only that I could but that I did float. Then I lost it and now I don't know if it was real.

NONE OF US DIED!"

Sounds like the British Isles.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-09T16:49:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-114323>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=114234)
>
> Astral projection? I have some very vivid if weird memories of this. For years I was convinced not only that I could but that I did float. Then I lost it and now I don't know if it was real.

This is out of body experiences?

"They" -- i.e. the sceptics -- have explanations for this. They say you unconsciously know what the view would be like if you were up above your body, and in some states of mind you imagine that you are and see what you know you would see. Sometimes I think people invent explanations for phenomena that disturb them because they don't fit into their view of the world -- and the world must be like such-and-such, mustn't it? There have been attempts to secrete objects on high shelves and try to get people to have an OBE and see if they saw them. I'm not sure that kind of thing isn't a bit beside the point, though. I do think these things happen but not necessarily to order and such that they could be reliably tested through repetition.

There's a couple of interesting mentions of an anthroposophist called Leo Baker who was up at Oxford with C. S. Lewis in Lewis's letters. In the the first, Lewis tells his friend Arthur Greeves back in Ireland that Baker claimed to have been clairvoyant since childhood and to have said that "anyone could compel a ghost to appear". Lewis said the discussion itself was a very strange experience, and he wasn't sure he hadn't been hypnotised. In the second letter Lewis says Baker said that when in the Royal Flying Corps in WWI he "was out of his body" and saw himself taking part in aerial combat and laughing maniacally.

I came across some even stranger material flicking through iTunes. There's an American Roman Catholic college that puts a lot of podcasts out there. I came across them looking for lectures on philosophy and philosophers, but they also have some far stranger material up -- including some rather far-fetched, but interesting, history lectures. (The speaker is a PhD from Cornell but he is a bit off-the-wall.) Some of the material is a bit politicised, too -- as seems to happen with religion in the U.S. sometimes. Anyway, there's a lecture by a psychologist who's RC and who seems to work with exorcists. She mentions some pretty odd stuff, like a person at a service of healing -- an Episcopal one not an RC one as it happens -- who supposedly levitated in the air and spun round.

No. 5 "Diabolical or Psychological" here:

[https://itunes.apple.com/itunes-u/guest-lectures/id418584170?mt=10](https://itunes.apple.com/itunes-u/guest-lectures/id418584170?mt=10)

Levitation would be something quite dramatic to see, if it can happen, wouldn't it?

### sooperb — 2014-03-09T17:00:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-114324>

I've always struggled with the concept of levitation; although I am not in any way a physics expert, such ability would defy the laws of gravity surely? I never discussed my thoughts that I could float with anyone, I suspect I realised that it wasn't/isn't possible and even more unlikely that I had achieved it. From what I have seen of the yogis who can apparently levitate, it is a description more of them being able to propel themselves into the air from a seated position rather than actually stay up in the air for any more time than a normal person would who simply jumped or launched themselves upwards from a chair or wall.

I was reading recently, ok it was in the Daily Mail lol, an account of a child who was picked up and tossed around by a poltergeist, witnessed allegedly by both a priest, a psychologist and a policeman. Policemen and priests don't lie do they lol and would a psychologist spot an opportunity to make some mileage from an unusual case study? Who knows.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-09T17:10:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-114325>

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-09T17:25:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-114329>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=114324)
>
> I've always struggled with the concept of levitation; although I am not in any way a physics expert, such ability would defy the laws of gravity surely? I never discussed my thoughts that I could float with anyone, I suspect I realised that it wasn't/isn't possible and even more unlikely that I had achieved it. From what I have seen of the yogis who can apparently levitate, it is a description more of them being able to propel themselves into the air from a seated position rather than actually stay up in the air for any more time than a normal person would who simply jumped or launched themselves upwards from a chair or wall.

I can't remember whether the psychologist in the podcast was present. I don't think she was. So, of course, there's a certain amount of room for misunderstanding, for growing in telling, and for something sounding more dramatic than it was. Still …

Sure, it's not the way objects generally behave in space. That they must _always_ is, of course, an assumption. It's not unreasonable to make that assumption, but it is an assumption.

> I was reading recently, ok it was in the Daily Mail lol, an account of a child who was picked up and tossed around by a poltergeist, witnessed allegedly by both a priest, a psychologist and a policeman. Policemen and priests don't lie do they lol and would a psychologist spot an opportunity to make some mileage from an unusual case study? Who knows.

Quite. This is a case where a determined sceptic would be thrown back on asserting that people were telling lies. One can always do that. But that that is what's going on doesn't always seem credible to me. I think there are a lot of cases where unusual phenomena occur and one can suggest "psychological explanations" for them, or that it was misunderstanding, or hypnosis or "collective hysteria" (where there are many witnesses), or suggestibility or any number of other things. But with something like this account you probably have to choose between collusion in deceit or acknowledging something unusual occurring.

AFAIK, poltergeist phenomena, despite the name, are often said to be not necessarily ghostly at all. It's said that they often occur where there is a disturbed teenager in a house -- something about their energy doing it.

### sooperb — 2014-03-09T17:35:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-114330>

"something about their energy doing it"

I've read that many times, but .... what kind of energy can move objects from a to b, sometimes with considerable force in similar circumstances (I know they call it kinetic energy but is there any credible explanation for this?). Strong winds tend not to blow across rooms apropos of nothing so what does that leave, some sort of electrical wave generated bodily but having a physical effect? I am interested in all these phenomena but I couldn't tell you whether or not I believe that they are actual or supposed, invented or real. I guess the detractors would say they are realists and that the believers are fantasists, I just don't see a credible explanation for or against. A bit like extra terrestials, they don't exist do they, yet the sightings and recordings date back centuries, well before space travel was even dreamt about.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-09T19:32:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-114339>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=114330)
>
> .... what kind of energy can move objects from a to b, sometimes with considerable force in similar circumstances (I know they call it kinetic energy but is there any credible explanation for this?)

Well, yeah, "kinetic" -- that's just from the Greek word for "movement". How could someone move something without touching it? I've no idea. And it's not everyday feature of our experience -- or it wouldn't seem strange or frightening to people.

But then I'm not sure how much of an understanding I have of anything. Once you start going back down into explanations of the physical world you arrive at the sub-atomic level and there you have -- what? -- _somethings_ that apparently behave somewhat like particles and somewhat like waves.

> I guess the detractors would say they are realists and that the believers are fantasists ...

Sure. But the joke's on them if they don't realise that all they really have are assumptions.

Of course, this kind of thing doesn't fit in easily with a view of the world that assumes that we are bound by "iron chains of causality". But in that world there really isn't any room for "persons" -- (and no-one ever smiles: there's just a twitching of muscles) -- yet we meet people every day.

The 2010 Gifford Lectures are quite interesting on these kinds of issues:

[http://www.st-andrews.ac.uk/gifford/category/2010/](http://www.st-andrews.ac.uk/gifford/category/2010/)

I don't think you can have definite proofs about the kind of world we live in nor about thing such as whether or not we have a purpose in it. People just have to make their choices based on how things seem to them.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-10T08:17:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-114477>

Why not? Robert O. Becker says that he thinks that electro-magnetic fields could explain all paranormal phenomena except for precognition.

Of course, if someone says to me, "What's electricity," I say, "A flow of electrons." And if they ask, "What's an electron?" I have to say, "Something I'm unable to conceive."

But it does relate events that are quite unusual to ones that are quite familiar -- such as walking in a room and clicking a switch on the wall for turn the light on.

\* \* \* \*

I was talking on the phone to my brother-in-law yesterday evening. He's got tickets for us to go to the Viking exhibition at the British Museum. Apparently, it's really good.

He was funniest on the cat, though. Apparently, they heard a squeal outside, and "he came in looking rather pleased with himself".

"I think he won that one," said Bill. I think he usually does.

He's not very cuddly for a cat. It was warm enough last Sunday for them to sit out on the patio for a few hours. The cat sat with them, as he likes to do. He won't sit on anyone's knee, though. He prefers his own chair.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-13T08:33:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-115133>

I woke up with cramp in right calf last night. Ouch!

I haven't had that for a while. I'd forgotten to take magnesium malate in the evening, and I suppose that was enough. I got up took two tablets and a mug of water and a good pinch of Celtic seasalt for good measure.

I suppose that symptoms of dizziness, brain-fog, orthostatic hypotension and whatnot that people often get when starting low carb are cause by the body dumping water and electrolytes and that's a passing thing. Cramp, however, still seems to lie in weight.

IIRC, one uses more magnesium when eating low carb, since it's needed for fat-burning. And, of course, it is depleted in soil these days. Two tablets of Source Naturals magnesium malate is my normal dose. That's two-thirds of the RDA. I don't take three tablets (there must be _some_ in my food!) I do also sometimes bathe in water with Epsom salts in, and occasionally use some "magnesium oil".

Funny. I never expected the cramp.

AFAIK, Jack has said that it is a standard problem with both Paleo and low carb diets and this is can also be linked to sub-optimal levels of iodine, since people on those diets generally don't know about the importance of seafood.

I had eaten fish twice that day and some brown shrimps, though. I'd also taken a kelp pill that morning. Oh well ...

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-14T10:58:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-115339>

I've nearly finished Eric Newby's _Love and War in the Apennines_, which has been said to be his masterpiece. It is a good read:

[http://www.amazon.com/Love-War-Apennines-Eric-Newby/dp/0007367899/](http://www.amazon.com/Love-War-Apennines-Eric-Newby/dp/0007367899/)

It's quite interesting, among other things, to read of people living in a way that wouldn't have changed much since medieval times, and this is only seventy years ago. There were people in parts of Europe still using candles for illumination and streams for water and with perhaps two suits of clothes. The world has changed in ways that would have been inconceivable for pre-War people.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-15T16:11:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-115589>

Quotation from Julian of Norwich:

> Though we sin continually he loves us endlessly, and so gently does he shew us our sin that we repent of it quietly, turning our mind to the contemplation of his mercy, clinging to his love and goodness, knowing that he is our cure, understanding that we do nothing but sin ... (For) if there be anywhere on earth a lover of God who is always kept safe from falling, I know nothing of it--for it was not shown me. But this was shown: that in faling and rising again we are always held close in one love.

Penguin translation from Julian's Middle English quoted in Robert Llewelyn's _With Pity Not with Blame._

More quotations:

[https://www.goodreads.com/author/quotes/156980.Julian\_of\_Norwich](https://www.goodreads.com/author/quotes/156980.Julian_of_Norwich)

This came into my mind listening to Vinnie Tortorich's book and podcast after he was a guest on Evan Brand's show.

Vinnie went to a convent school, and he says the nuns were extremely brutal. They put bricks on his hands. They also told him he would have an "indelible" stain on his soul, if he had "bad thoughts" about girls. I was quite surprised. I've rarely encountered nuns, and none like these. All this doesn't seem anything like the Man from Nazereth. One wonders: did they have a religious vocation or was it perhaps something people went into out of economic necessity or something back in those days? Well, no, not quite that, I suppose, because they evidently believed this "indelible stain" nonsense.

Julian also says:

> I saw full surely that wherever our Lord appears, peace reigns, and anger has no place. For I saw no whit of anger in God in short or in long term.

Some religions seem to be fairly brutal, but this is _supposed_ to be a religion where forgiveness matters. Of course, Lady Julian isn't taking a merely "liberal" view of wrongdoing as a modern person might--that it just doesn't matter. (One thinks of Peter Simple's Dr. Heinz Kiosk.) It matters all right. But it can be forgiven. "Though we sin continually he loves us endlessly".

And again the attitude to sexual matters of these nuns is a bit disturbing. This can easily tend towards "Manichaeism" where it's seen as to do with the body and hence "matter" which is regarded as evil. That's certainly not Christian. I'm religiously sceptic myself, but I just don't see where these nuns were coming from and what they thought it had to do with Christianity.

Now I'm not advocating some kind of sexual free-for-all. Sex is a difficult area -- it connects with very deep areas inside one. So it's certainly possible for people to be hurt very badly by erotic love, and we should not give others pain. But there's nothing instrinsically amiss with it: it's more that it's better that it happen in the right way and work at all levels of the personality.

This upbringing seems to have had a wholly bad effect on Vinnie. He has some interesting things to say in his podcast, but he spends an inordinate amount of time trying to be shocking in a sexual manner -- like a naughty little boy trotting out all his rude words to get attention. The effect is to make sex seem rather trivial if not sordid. And, of course, it's neither of these things. It's the root of some of the most moving poetry in the world; it can causes us to forget their own small selves (at least for a time). It even seems to people to have transcendental significance -- to point beyond this limited everyday existence we have, which is why it demands vows and ceremonies. It matters. It's like Vinnie's still revenging himself for things that happened to him as a teenager on people who are long dead.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-16T14:32:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-115765>

I should add I'm not mocking peoples' religious beliefs.

I find people like Julian of Norwich and Dorothy Kerin very impressive. In fact, being off for a few days this week, I'm popping across to Burrswood to see the hospital Dorothy Kerin founded there, the gardens and grounds, and the chapel Dorothy had built and furnished, mostly with Italian Rennaissance artwork that she went across and found herself in Italy. Burrswood seems to have been a very special place, and there are some interesting accounts of the place. One, incidentally, does involve a nun. Apparently, she was sent there as a last resort and in a bad way. She, like some others, was not able to be healed, either by the medical care given there or by the prayers Dorothy offered. However, she arrived feeling very bitter, but apparently found an overwhelming sense of peace and happiness. Better to find it at the end of one's life than never. Apparently, Dorothy said that the most important thing was to find spiritual healing, and you might be given physical healing for a condition afterwards but not necessarily.

We may also pop into All Saints Church at Tudeley, which is fairly near there, and where there is some Chagall glass:

[http://www.tudeley.org/allsaintstudeley.htm](http://www.tudeley.org/allsaintstudeley.htm)

So ... I'm not anti-religious.

In fact, if anyone would like me to light a candle at Burrswood let me know.

### sooperb — 2014-03-16T15:24:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-115770>

"like a naughty little boy trotting out all his rude words"

Akin to Billy Connolly. His humour was hilarious but his language wasn't, a little you can accept for emphasis but as the years rolled there were more expletives than gags. I gave him up.

Have you seen Philomena Mike? It is by all accounts a pretty good example of the sympathetic nature of nuns, total lack of any empathy for the suffering of others. I don't if it was just certain religious orders that used God's word as permission to punish all sinners or whether it was widespread. I know a lot of women who were educated by nuns, they all recount the lack of any sort of kindness and the fervour with which they inflicted physical punishment on any wrong doing. Being God's instrument, in their case, seems to have involved a great deal of the use of pain as a punishment. It can't all be hearsay and urban myth.

I don't have any religious beliefs, too much suffering is caused in the name of religion for it to work for me. I believe in the human condition, compassion, kindness and a helping hand wherever it's needed and you can supply it. You don't have to be religious to help your fellow man.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-17T20:01:15+0200

I really dislike unsolicited phone calls myself. At least this one was unintentionally amusing.

### sooperb — 2014-03-17T20:48:31+0200

Well known scam, she's lucky, they can be very persuasive if you aren't aware. It makes me chuckle when Indian call centre operatives use an English name when they answer, who they think they're fooling, "Hi, this is Steve, how can I help you". He's not a Steve in a million years, why pretend? I'm just as happy to speak to Abdul or Mohammed.

Another curiosity is that Chinese women who come to Britain, always adopt an English Christian name and it's often the more exotic too. Jessica, Joanne, Cheryl, I've met a lot lol.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-22T08:26:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-117102>

I came across a bottle of kombucha in a large health food store a couple of days ago, so I bought it. I'm not sure it's a good idea to make it yourself -- at least in this mild damp climate -- because of the problem of mould infections. But buying it -- I guess a commercial concern would watch for that.

I'd guess that lactic-fermented vegetables are probably a safer option for home fermentation, because all the salt should encouage the right micro-organisms and discourage the wrong ones.

Dr. Art Ayers at Cooling Inflammation mentioned a new book on this that's coming out soon:

[http://2.bp.blogspot.com/-B7n-hp5Zfcs/UwZ7rLXS4JI/AAAAAAAAAqk/ZrDJMqbSc5Y/s1600/Fermented.jpg](http://2.bp.blogspot.com/-B7n-hp5Zfcs/UwZ7rLXS4JI/AAAAAAAAAqk/ZrDJMqbSc5Y/s1600/Fermented.jpg)

### sooperb — 2014-03-22T20:49:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-117214>

I just don't like the standard British pub, harks back to when I worked in one and people smoked, I went home kippered and everything stunk, my hair, my skin, my clothes. Most town pubs just seem dedicated to drinking, another item on my "off" list lol, they are often dirty, either too warm or too cold and the food, I'd rather go hungry! As a non-drinker I've spent many an evening watching people slowly get drunk (or quickly in the case of my acquaintance Shirley), it's like watching a werewolf transformation, one minute they seem sober and the next they're slurring words, tipping over and sometimes throwing up, it's like they don't have an off switch and just keep going until they conk out or empty out.

Country pubs seem to try a bit harder and can be cosy and inviting, not so many drunks as most folk seem to arrive in a car, even the food can be acceptable. I like to drink wine with a meal but it wrecks my sleep so often that I tend to stick with water.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-23T21:29:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-117443>

I just read a biography of Helen Keller -- began it around midday yesterday and finished it around the same time today:

[http://www.amazon.com/Helen-Keller-Life-Dorothy-Herrmann/dp/0226327639/](http://www.amazon.com/Helen-Keller-Life-Dorothy-Herrmann/dp/0226327639/)

I was using a quotation of hers about "security" being "mostly a superstition" -- one I'd come across years ago being used by a computer security specialist. I knew little about the lady, though, merely that she was left deaf-blind by disease when a toddler and had to learn to engage with the world through touch.

Apparently, she was about the best-known and most highly revered American woman of her age, though she had some skeletons in the closet. (Don't biographers love these? This one's only complaint was that as papers had been destroyed in a house-fire there may have been some that were unfortunately lost.) Helen was, I'm afraid, an admirer of the Soviet Union, and asked to name the three greatest men of the age replied Lenin, Edison and Charlie Chaplin, which is about as silly (and sinister) an answer as one could give. Of course, people were more naive in those days … but then they also did choose not to believe reports coming out of Russia.

Helen was remarkably beautiful as a young woman -- on the left here with her teacher Annie Sullivan:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

There's a couple of interesting snippets about her sense of touch in the book. Apparently, she considered she could tell how intelligent a person was from the feel of the musculature in their hand, and could tell how a person was feeling by touch, despite their trying to hide their feelings. (But she said that there was something personal in the _tonus_ that remained through different emotions.)

She was also interesting on George Bernard Shaw. She met him in London. When she held his hand, she said it was "like a thistle" and claimed she could detect the most massive ego behind how it felt. Shaw made a crude and heartless joke about her disabilities, which he later claimed was meant to break the ice. What I know of Shaw she had him pegged right.

An interesting book.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-03-24T03:24:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-117512>

> [Gagnrad said:](/goto/post?id=117443)
>
> I just read a biography of Helen Keller -- began it around midday yesterday and finished it around the same time today:
>
> [http://www.amazon.com/Helen-Keller-Life-Dorothy-Herrmann/dp/0226327639/](http://www.amazon.com/Helen-Keller-Life-Dorothy-Herrmann/dp/0226327639/)
>
> I was using a quotation of hers about "security" being "mostly a superstition" -- one I'd come across years ago being used by a computer security specialist. I knew little about the lady, though, merely that she was left deaf-blind by disease when a toddler and had to learn to engage with the world through touch.
>
> Apparently, she was about the best-known and most highly revered American woman of her age, though she had some skeletons in the closet. (Don't biographers love these? This one's only complaint was that as papers had been destroyed in a house-fire there may have been some that were unfortunately lost.) Helen was, I'm afraid, an admirer of the Soviet Union, and asked to name the three greatest men of the age replied Lenin, Edison and Charlie Chaplin, which is about as silly (and sinister) an answer as one could give. Of course, people were more naive in those days … but then they also did choose not to believe reports coming out of Russia.
>
> Helen was remarkably beautiful as a young woman -- on the left here with her teacher Annie Sullivan:
>
>
>
>
> There's a couple of interesting snippets about her sense of touch in the book. Apparently, she considered she could tell how intelligent a person was from the feel of the musculature in their hand, and could tell how a person was feeling by touch, despite their trying to hide their feelings. (But she said that there was something personal in the _tonus_ that remained through different emotions.)
>
> She was also interesting on George Bernard Shaw. She met him in London. When she held his hand, she said it was "like a thistle" and claimed she could detect the most massive ego behind how it felt. Shaw made a crude and heartless joke about her disabilities, which he later claimed was meant to break the ice. What I know of Shaw she had him pegged right.
>
> An interesting book.

the touch angle is a big feature in the March webinar......

Very, very interesting. I LOVE this stuff.... touch... how to feel people... why my belly feel.. what is this all. I want to know more.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-24T09:13:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-117548>

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=117503)
>
> All I remember about Helen Keller was that she was taught to speak using a balloon, her teacher was a bit of a sadist and that Helen did not transform into a likeable, sweet woman, she was pretty unpleasant. I may be a bit off in my recollections though.

This biography doesn't accuse Annie Sullivan of sadism. And it seems that Helen Keller was very fond of her. The best the biographer could do was to reprint a couple of dreams where she (the biographer) said that Helen was expressing a suppressed aggressive attitude towards Annie. It's not really clear that they do. I expect there was some tension at times, if never publicly expressed, but more affection and dependence (on both sides).

But Annie was extremely demanding and sometimes very physical with Helen when she was a small girl -- forcing a spoon into her hand, that kind of thing.

I don't think Helen was unpleasant. Again the best the biographer can do -- biographers love to tear people down, don't they? -- was to suggest that she had to be too much of a "secular saint". IOW, she was uncomplaining about her lot, generally cheerful, and helpful and energetic in charity work and campaigns for the blind.

I just found the revolutionary socialism a little creepy. But this is hindsight, of course. I do see that many people then did see it as some kind of solution to real problems ... though I'm sure the darker side has always been evident for those who have eyes to see:

[http://www.amazon.com/Devils-Oxford-Worlds-Classics-Dostoevsky/dp/0199540497/](http://www.amazon.com/Devils-Oxford-Worlds-Classics-Dostoevsky/dp/0199540497/)

But she toned it down in later years not wanting to put contributors to her charities off.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-24T09:17:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-117550>

> [Inger said:](/goto/post?id=117542)
>
> Very, very interesting. I LOVE this stuff.... touch... how to feel people... why my belly feel.. what is this all. I want to know more.

Yes, all this fascinates me.

It's something else that makes one wonder about the body/mind distinction. Even if they are two things and not one, at any rate they're so bound together that for all practical purposes they're like two different sides of the same thing.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-25T22:27:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-117901>

Here's another picture. This one's of a pump-clip taken in the pub at the weekend. I had a glass of cider -- last drink I'll have for a few days I think. I didn't have any stout. It was a temptation in a way, though -- not often you see stout these days that is actually "real ale" -- i.e. brewed all naturally and not pasteurised, filtered, chilled, or injected with nitrogen, but left live and served on handpump:

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-25T22:42:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-117904>

Interesting that stout -- which is to say stout (i.e. "strong") porter -- is associated so strongly with Ireland nowadays.

AFAIK, porter was originally _the_ London beer. It was what the London porters -- the men who carried meat and vegetables in the markets like Smithfield drank -- hence the name. Men doing physical work, like the porters, apparently liked these darker beers, because they were generally stronger and sweeter, supplying more calories. And I guess they didn't worry about their figures.

London water, which is soft, is good for darker beers (as is the water of the River Liffey in Dublin). Burton-on-Trent, which has harder water, was famed for it's paler and bitterer beers.

The First World War helped kill the preference for darker beers. The British Government decided that fuel could not be spared to roast the malt darker for them, so brewers were told not to for the duration. At this time, Ireland was part of the United Kingdom, but the political situation there was too sensitive for the beer to be interfered with, so Irish brewers were exempted from the ban on roasting malt dark. Hence, perhaps, people carried on being used to drinking porter there.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-27T20:43:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-118343>

Talking of drink, here's a winery where some of the best "champagne" around is made. They're not allowed to call it that, but the grape varieties and methods are the same, as is the geology. It's beaten top French champagnes in blind tastings. I visited it once. The winemaker said to us that he doesn't fool with trying to make red wines, because the climate's just not suitable, but whites work very well.

[http://www.breakybottom.co.uk](http://www.breakybottom.co.uk)

Lunch was a fairly big dressed salad with a few anchovies, a small handful of nuts, and a small piece of some 100% cacao dark chocolate.

Dinner: I fried up some shitake mushrooms in clarified butter and olive oil. Pretty good. I followed that with about a quarter of a pound of fried liver and then some lightly cooked brussels sprouts with black pepper and seasalt and some olive oil drizzled on them. A glass of red wine. I also had a little dark chocolate.

I decided I would take a drink, though I'd been meaning not to for a while. I think I'll go dry for the whole of the month of April instead.

Feeling pretty good. Yesterday I found my belt buckle would do up on the old hole where it used to be before I drifted. More or less back to normal, although it took almost three months.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-28T08:17:45+0200

I'll have a look at those this weekend.

### sooperb — 2014-03-28T11:46:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-118451>

Earthing is a worthwhile read, I liked it because Clinton Ober didn't start out looking for anything, he stumbled across it and from then on worked on his theory, arrived at a conclusion that, to me, was an extremely valuable one for a lot of people and then set about spreading the word. I think he's right up there with the greats, perhaps not a Nobel prize winner but he deserves more recognition than he seems to have gained.

Another read which I can recommend is Jeff T Bowles, "The Miraculous results of Extremely High Doses Of Vit. D3" he is clearly a bit loopy but well meaning. The bibliography is longer almost than the book, he's read an awful lot of research. Interesting.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-30T10:25:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-118783>

I also saw a grey heron yesterday.

Breakfast: avocado, lamb's liver and kidneys marinated in lemon juice and fried in butter, scrambled egg made from 1 whole egg, 1 egg yolk and 1 tbs coconut oil, sauerkraut, cup of black coffee.

FWIW, FitDay indicates: 849Kcal (71% fat, 18% protein, 11% carbs).

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-30T10:31:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-118785>

Mothering Sunday today. Who remembered?

The supermarket had shedloads of flowers in the aisle yesterday. For some weeks they've had a large display of bottles of various different drinks, boxes of chocolates, and rather repellent stuffed toys. Do they think people's mothers are childish, chocolate-gorging alcoholics or something?

And don't start me on the cards. I could never buy most of what they have on offer. The artwork and the sentiments of many of them are so sentimental they scream _insincere_ at you. But maybe it's just me.

### Gagnrad — 2014-03-30T10:45:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-118789>

The latest Sally Fallon/Thomas Cowan book continues to prove intriguing.

Here's a strange snippet. They quote a doctor who says that the child has 20 teeth (4 sets of 5), whereas the adult has (or rather should have) 32 (4 sets of 8). This doctor says these correspond to the two main scales used in Western music: the pentatonic, which is used in folk music (and lullabies); and the octave scale, which is used in classical music.

Now you don't prove a connection between two phenomena (or theories) merely by gesturing at some kind of similarity between them.

However, I think this is interesting as showing, here and elsewhere, how much influence Rudolf Steiner (who they also quote as saying the teeth correspond in some way to the soul) has had on the WAPF. Not saying that's a bad thing; just noticing it. (But I think it would shock many people in Paleo and confirm them in dismissing the WAPF, as many do.)

I think it would be less controversial and fair to say, as they do, that it would be best to play music suitable for children's state of development to them. "Mozart for babies" CDs are probably not what they need or want. Similarly, it makes one wonder if adults who have not gained some appreciation of classical music -- there are plenty of them -- are in a state of arrested development.

I feel that we must put music back into our lifes, BIG WAY!

We have become lost in the world of harmonics today... Some generations have not been exposed to tones at all, by that, digital media is not made for humans, it is for androids. I have always hated all of these "for dummies" things we do. Like you said, remake music to a easy digestive form, just like fastfoods, do not feed humans.

Since we slowly killed music in schools, i see a small trend now that it is gaining powers again. Many schools where i live have their classes full.

I think, instead of those iPads, iPhones and other stuff, supply your kids with a wide spectrum of analog instruments so they can explore by themself without OUR opinions... This is what school is missing BIG TIME today, at least here.

With you all the way, Peter.

Over here when they planned out a National Curriculum and wrote up documents that were supposed to ensure a good standard of education at all British schools guess what were the last two Curriculum Documents to be drawn up?

Music and Physical Education. They didn't think they were important. The two things that were thought to be most vital in education by the Ancient Greeks. Over here they tend to assume that all that really matters is the "3Rs" (reading, writing and arithmetic) all of which they introduce to the children too early. Anything else seems to be seen as a bit of decoration around the edges.

I remember my school times... When we came to some parts of the season, everyone started to squirm and skip classes, why?

It was time for music and dance classes... Boy, this was heaven for me. Early in my life i found motion, when school started i found girls, then when you got a free chance to connect all these elements, heaven is a place on earth...

I never understood why my friends hated this, to touch girls for free? What kinf of guy would not like that? It was like i was left in a candystore with a free hour to spend.

### sooperb — 2014-03-30T12:23:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-118805>

I can only agree with both of you, some music moves me to tears, which I think is a good thing, good old fashioned stuff with melody and tones that meld together and play my soul. Modern music, pah, very little that doesn't just fly past my ears and disappear without leaving an imprint.

My daughter god bless her, eschewed TV, won't have one, and despite being as poor as a church mouse has had both her girls learn an instrument. One is gifted musically and plays the harp and piano and could probably play anything if she chose too. The other plays the piano and has learnt the violin and viola, both can sing too. They were both forbidden Facebook accounts but are allowed phones although she hates them having them. One is an avid reader, the other reads but not to the same degree. I tried piano lessons with the younger two but they wouldn't practice so it was abandoned after about a year. My son taught himself acoustic guitar but moved on to electric. My younger daughter was a beautiful singer but she just can't do it in public which is a shame but I completely understand.

I also agree that children are at school far too young, at 4 they are still babies. I am totally against them going even earlier, it's fast becoming a child care option. Why have children if you don't want to raise them? My niece, late to motherhood, had her first at 38 and followed with another not too long after, the boy was at pre-school as soon as he was three and the girl has just been given a place for when she is three next January. You'd think that after becoming a mother so late she would have wanted to spend as much time with her babies as she could but apparently not.

> [sooperb said:](/goto/post?id=118805)
>
> I also agree that children are at school far too young, at 4 they are still babies. I am totally against them going even earlier, it's fast becoming a child care option. Why have children if you don't want to raise them? My niece, late to motherhood, had her first at 38 and followed with another not too long after, the boy was at pre-school as soon as he was three and the girl has just been given a place for when she is three next January. You'd think that after becoming a mother so late she would have wanted to spend as much time with her babies as she could but apparently not.

Most respectfully, I must disagree here, Sue.

There so many advantages to a child’s early education. When my daughter entered a kindergarten at age of 5, she was among only a handful there who could already read, write and do do basic arithmetics, even though it was one of the more affluent neighborhoods. She and those others few had a huge advantage in their continuing progress. As good as the rest of them were doing, most of them could never catch up with them.

Also, it is so important for them to start interacting with the other children as soon as possible - not just arranging a playdates for them or bringing them to a playground, but to have them together in the learning environment.

I grew up in the place and the time when all the mothers had no choice in the matter and had to work. Unless, there were grandparents who could take care of their grandchildren, they all had to be left in the nurseries and kindergartens. Putting aside the political indoctrination of it, I had greatly benefitted from that experience.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-02T08:31:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-119369>

> [Tanya said:](/goto/post?id=119251)
>
> I guess, it is a little bit different in here. In addition to playing piano already, she learned to play violin in her school. Their physical education classes were pretty good as well. What some of the children were doing when they got home - was a different story....

I have heard U.S. sources say that PE and sports have died back a lot in schools over the last decade or two till in some cases it's virtually absent. I suppose they're accurate -- but then it's a big country and the situation's probably different in different states.

Our policy is now national (or is intended to be) -- the National Curriculum, now over twenty years old, was supposed to solve all our educational problems. Needless to say it hasn't. There is a PE document; but, as I say, it was one of the last drawn up, which I think is an indication of the perceived relative importance of the subject. Some schools have sold off their playing fields to raise money, which is another indication.

I suppose it was something of an exaggeration when I said:

> The two things that were thought to be most vital in education by the Ancient Greeks

I think there's something in that. But "Gymnastic" was partly important as military training, since the City-States were often at war with one another. And I think "Music" also included what we'd now class as literature.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-02T08:33:34+0300

I've found a new way to eat coconut oil. I do not like the taste.

I heat a mugful of beef broth and dissolve a tablespoon in it. With salt and pepper and turmeric added as well, the coconut flavour is no longer obvious or offensive.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-02T10:40:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-119379>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=119375)
>
> MCT oil has no flavour ........

Thanks. I didn't know that. I've never tried it.

I do prefer whole-foods on the whole. Sometimes you lose useful components of a food by refining it. For example, the lauric acid is not going to be in the MCT oil. This is the fatty acid that's fairly unusual. It is in human breast milk and is antiviral, antimicrobial, antiprotozoal and antifungal.

But I can see that MCT oil is useful for those who want to get into ketosis quickly.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-06T17:25:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-120064>

[](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/img_0142-jpg.397/)

This is the jawbone of a young man from a grave near Weymouth in Dorset. He's filed his teeth, which apparently some vikings did. The note on the display case suggests that perhaps pigment was rubbed into the groove to add ferocity to the man's appearance.

I do think it a little ghoulish to display human remains to people to gawp at in a museum. I was interested to look at it, however. I didn't actually look for the filing. What I wanted to see was whether all the teeth that had been recovered looked sound (so far as one could tell) and whether they'd all come through. I expected that both things would be true, and they were. I counted around the bottom set of teeth and made 16. Top and bottom jaw look like they'd meet, so that makes 32. Hardly anyone nowadays has _all_ their teeth emerging, and straight. It amazes me that dentists and orthodontists don't, on the whole, notice this or think about it. It's quite clearly down to the food -- as Weston Price showed (a long time ago now).

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-08T20:12:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-120408>

I've been enjoying another Eric Newby book. _Love and War in the Appenines_ was very good, but so is this:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Round-Ireland-Gear-Eric-Newby/dp/0007367929/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/Round-Ireland-Gear-Eric-Newby/dp/0007367929/)

He toured on a bicycle accompanied by what the blurb on the back describes as "his long-suffering wife" Wanda. Poor Wanda was frequently exhausted, soaked in heavy rain, splattered with mud and once blown off her bike and into a ditch by a high wind. I haven't yet finished the book, but I believe after this trip she said to him she'd never go on a cycling holiday with him again. Memo to wives: probably avoid going cycling with a 6′ 2″ husband who sailed before the mast as a teenager, hiked the Hindu Kush and was in the Special Boat Section in the War.

Ireland in the 1980s sounds like a fascinating and attractive place. So did the Italy Eric Newby wrote about in the earlier book. It's interesting in a different way. Life in the Apennines _circa_ 1944 seems almost like something out of the far past, besides being a place where they do things differently. But the descriptions of Ireland in the 80s seems both close and remote at the same time. It seems not so different from England in the 80s: there's a similar climate, similar countryside (if much less spoiled), similar foods, ubiquitous tea … and, of course, there's the same habit of drinking too much beer. And yet it sounds very different, too.

Newby mentions a kind of black pudding that he says Irish ex-patriates were said to come back to Ireland just to taste again. It's called _drisheen_ and used to be made with sheeps' blood -- they use pigs' now -- or the blood of geese. I remember @[[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted]) saying on the boards here that he's had puddings made from poultry blood.

There's one area of Ireland that he says is so full of ancient remains that:

> I experienced a feeling of unreality about the twentieth century that I had hitherto experienced nowhere else in the western world. I find it impossible to analyse, or even begin to write about it, but the memory remains with me still. … even the minds of scholars must reel ...

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-08T20:28:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-120412>

The sound of silence …

St. Peter's Church in East Blatchington, East Sussex has recorded the silence within the church and made it available as a CD at a modest price to cover production costs. It's not, of course, total silence: there's apparently the sound of floorboards creaking, people moving in the pews, distant traffic outside. This, I suppose, is the point: that it could be used as a kind of meditational aid, giving one a focus to listen to.

The CD seems to have sold out, and they're now offering a DVD that puts the "silent" track to images of the 800-year-old church.

[http://stpeterandstandrew.btck.co.uk/SaintPetersEastBlatchington/TheSilenceBeautyofStPetersCDDVD](http://stpeterandstandrew.btck.co.uk/SaintPetersEastBlatchington/TheSilenceBeautyofStPetersCDDVD)

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-sussex-19978247](http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/uk-england-sussex-19978247)

Apparently, someone in Ireland heard it and made recordings in something like five different ancient Irish abbeys and churches, making them available on CD.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-09T08:39:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-120530>

Only a few chapters to go of _Round Ireland in Low Gear_. I've just got hold of another Eric Newby book:

> This book is a lush and beautiful memoir of a very special house and a superb recreation of a bygone era.
>
> In 1967, veteran travel writer Eric Newby and his heroic wife Wanda fulfiled their dream of a return to life in the Italian hills where they first met during World War II. But this fulfilment would not come easy. The dream materialised in the form of I Castagni ('The Chestnuts'), a small, decrepit farmhouse with no roof, an abandoned septic tank and its own indigenous wildlife reluctant to give up their home. But in the foothills of the Apuan Alps on the border of Liguria and Northern Tuscany, this ramshackle house would soon become a hub of love, friendship and activity.
>
> Whether recounting dangerous expeditions through Afghanistan or everyday life in a country house, Newby's talent shines through as one of the foremost writers of the comic travel genre. Full of Newby's sharp wit and good humour, ‘A Small Place’ in Italy returns, twenty years later, to the life of Newby's much-cherished classic, Love and War in the Apennines. It lovingly recounts the quickly disappearing lifestyle of the idiosyncratic locals, and the enduring friendships they forge, whether sharing in growing their first wine harvest as novices or frying poisonous mushrooms for a feast.

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Small-Place-Italy-Eric-Newby/dp/0007367902/](http://www.amazon.co.uk/Small-Place-Italy-Eric-Newby/dp/0007367902/)

It sounds really good.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-10T20:51:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-120829>

I just reached a point in the Eric Newby book where he mentions an old lady, a Mrs Conolly, in her 90s who was an 18th century Dublin society hostess. One letter writer of the time mentions that she would still rise (relatively) early, would do her own business, and could read a newspaper by candlelight without the aid of glasses.

Who has eyesight like that these days at 90? Wouldn't it be interesting to know what food she was brought up on? No fear of fat in those days, and I suppose all the vegetables were organic and well-manured (because they'd have had to be) and all the livestock was pretty much free-range and grass-fed (if naturally grass-eating).

There's no hint of what she did eat, except the interesting note that she always dined at 3 (the old dinner hour) and "if the greatest person in the kingdom dined with her never altered her bill of fare".

### Lahelada — 2014-04-10T22:38:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-120857>

Made me look ...a lively lady by any standard "His wife continued to act as a political hostess throwing lavish parties at Castletown to perpetuate her husbands memory. Castletown became a fashionable place to be seen and was regarded as a rival to the Vice-Regal Court in Dublin Castle. In the early 1730s Katherine effectively ran a casino in the house when the Lord Lieutenant Dorset banned gambling. Carriage races along the avenue were also a feature of Castletown entertainments and Katherine long partook fully in these activities with a host of rakish young men. Upon her death in 1752, at the age of ninety, it was lamented that the huge house that used to be crowded with guests of all sorts was empty and forlorn. Mrs Delany's tribute upon her death in 1752 gives some indication of Katherine Conolly's virtues as a hostess, "We have lost our great Mrs Conolly She died last Friday and is a general loss. Her table was open to all friends of all ranks and her purse to the poor. She was I think in her ninetieth year. She had been dropping for some years, but never too ill to shut out company."

Her letters and her household accounts are meticulous so you could look into her diet if you wished
from [http://www.castletown.ie/About/TheConollyFamilyandCastletown/](http://www.castletown.ie/About/TheConollyFamilyandCastletown/)

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-13T16:04:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-121262>

I had a three-course breakfast -- I suppose you could call it that. LOL

A Manx cure kipper
A plateful of spring greens stewed slowly with onion, butter, chicken stock and Toulouse sausage
Some Flower Marie cheese

One can eat well on paleo. Perhaps this is why so-called experts -- like the staring-eyed zealot David Katz who votes it the worst \[_sic_\] diet every year -- badmouth it so much. It's sheer envy.

But have pity for those poor prunes munching through bowls of All-Bran, deludedly thinking it does them good. There used to be a heart surgeon who cycled the London to Brighton road every year wearing a T-shirt bearing the legend "All-Bran for Brekkers". The cycling shows some spirit, but the breakfast? … Rather him than me.

I had a couple of months avoiding all dairy products, but it doesn't seem to have made any difference to anything, so I'm no longer "full paleo". Hence the Flower Marie, and very good it was, too.

Lunch was:

Dressed avocado and a few olives
A casserole of mixed game meats
A glass of organic cider from the Welsh Marches
A few nuts

Oh, dear … all that fat will "clog my arteries"

I'll probably have a large dressed salad and tin of sardines tonight.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-13T16:28:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-121266>

This weekend I managed to finish _Round Ireland in Low Gear_ and start _A Small Place in Italy_. I've now finished the latter. Eric Newby never disappoints.

Newby was good on the Isles of Arran. When he first visited there in the 1960s, people were still fishing from coracles and wearing a kind of traditional costume. He says they had heel-less shoes (sensible chaps) made from cowhide with the hair turned outwards; thick tweed trousers, slit up the sides, so that they could be rolled up for wading, thick blue flannel shirts with "stand-up collars", woven waistcoats, and large flat caps. By the 80s only the caps remained. The women had beautiful long hand-crocheted shawls, also still worn at that time on parts of the Irish mainland. It would cost you a fortune to buy such a shawl now.

To grow anything there, people had to put down alternate layers of sand and seaweed to make soil, carted on donkeys by pannier. It seems like it must have made pretty rich soil, though, because Newby comments on the lushness of the grass the cattle were eating.

The inhabitants at this time were tall and strong -- and Gaelic-speaking, although it's thought they may be, in part at any rate, descended from English troops that garrisoned the islands at one time. They also spoke quietly -- "voices as soft as the wind," Newby says. I've heard this of people living on islands before. Interesting to compare this with the way some city people talk -- bellow like bulls.

_A Small Place in Italy_ is a lovely book. It's the story of a disused and very run-down farmhouse that Eric Newby and his wife Wanda bought and restored in the part of Italy where he'd been a prisoner of war during WWII. This is where he met Wanda, who helped him while he was on the run from the Germans.

### Gagnrad — 2014-04-14T19:44:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-121453>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=121404)
>
> Mike - what do you pay for that beautiful cheese? I really didn't consider that cheese isn't paleo lol!
>
> I didn't for a long time .... but now - I have to have English Stilton .... or I would sulk for quite some time!

£7:50. It's a fair-sized block, but that is a pretty stiff price. Still, the ewes don't give so much milk, and I don't think they can milk them all year round, unlike cows -- or cows as farmers have bred them over the centuries. And it's all non-intensive farming. You get what you pay for, I suppose. And I suppose I can't always pass it up and then complain at the food-supply system being non-quality orientated and careless of the land, human health, and animal welfare. I can get Manchego much cheaper, although it's coming here from La Mancha, but I suppose that's done on a larger scale. I've seen Greek goats' milk cheese labelled as both organic and raw selling for even less, but I think that's done on a larger scale again.

Here's the farm. They do goats' as well as sheeps' cheese:

[http://www.goldencrosscheese.co.uk](http://www.goldencrosscheese.co.uk)

I've already cut a piece off the cheese twice, but I think I should really have left it to mature a bit longer.

As for cheese not being paleo -- well, no, Cordain has ruled it out. He likes to say "you try catching a wild animal and milking it". I think he's missing something here, though. There are accounts of Plains Indians, when in a hurry, killing a buffalo calf in order to eat the contents of its stomach -- effectively, curds. They were given to children as a treat, too. It's described, for example, here:

[http://www.amazon.com/The-Captured-Abduction-Indians-Frontier/dp/0312317891/](http://www.amazon.com/The-Captured-Abduction-Indians-Frontier/dp/0312317891/)

I think we can surmise that other hunting peoples have likely done the same.

Cordain is an indefatigable and hard-working scientist. He's at home with biochemistry journals. Cordain's trouble is (a) he doesn't actually know much of the ethnographic material, and (b) because he wouldn't eat the stomach contents of an animal himself it's never occurred to him that hunter-gatherers might act differently.

### caroline — 2014-04-14T23:51:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gagnrads-journal.9399/post-121504>
